dimensions.summary: The Dead Code dimension is broadly healthy in its essentials but carries a meaningful tail of accumulated cruft, with most findings rated low severity and the codebase showing no evidence of dead logic in critical runtime paths. The clearest signal is post-refactor residue: an orphaned `better-sqlite3` dependency and a vestigial RNG call-count tracker left behind after the server/SQLite-store removal, plus fully unused exported modules (`replay.ts`, the testing `fixtures.ts` ScenarioBuilder suite, and a cluster of designer helpers, debug/assertion exports, and the `useHoverHighlight` composable) that appear to be speculative or abandoned API surface with zero consumers. More concerning are a few medium-severity items that risk active confusion rather than mere bloat: a dead, divergent duplicate of `generateCandidateFeatures()` sharing an identical name but different logic, ~85 lines of verbatim-duplicated lobby session callbacks across `GameSession.create`/`restore`, game-option metadata types duplicated as two sources of truth across session and types, and an orphaned `ParallelTrainer` subtree documented as the primary training API yet driven by no command or consumer. Smaller hygiene issues round it out — no-op stubs (`saveAICode()`), dead private helpers, a self-importing barrel that creates circular dependencies, and CLI flags (`validate --fix/--skip-simulation`, `lint --fix`) accepted but silently ignored. None of these block functionality, but the divergent-duplicate and silently-ignored-flag findings undercut the "pit of success" principle and should be prioritized over the larger volume of simple unused-export removals.

findings.title: Dead, divergent duplicate of generateCandidateFeatures() in feature-templates.ts (different logic, identical name)
findings.evidence: src/ai-trainer/feature-templates.ts:2446 exports `generateCandidateFeatures(structure)`. A SECOND function with the same name lives at src/ai-trainer/feature-generator.ts:7. Every real consumer imports the feature-generator version: src/ai-trainer/index.ts:15 re-exports from './feature-generator.js'; benchmark-worker.ts:10, simulation-worker.ts:14, parallel-trainer.ts:20 all `import { generateCandidateFeatures } from './feature-generator.js'`. Nothing imports `from './feature-templates'` except `FEATURE_TEMPLATES`/`FeatureTemplate` (feature-generator.ts:2, index.ts:22). The two bodies differ substantively: feature-generator dedups via `seenIds` and uses `templateApplies()`; feature-templates.ts:2452 filters on `matchesGameType(...winConditionInfo.gameType)` and does NO dedup.
findings.whyConcern: Two exported functions share one name but implement different filtering/dedup semantics. The feature-templates.ts copy is never called, so any maintenance applied there (e.g. adding a new template-applicability rule) silently has zero effect while looking correct.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A developer or AI agent editing feature generation will land on whichever copy grep finds first and may 'fix' the dead one, shipping a no-op change. Identical names defeat the single-source-of-truth principle.
findings.fixPaths: Delete the unused `generateCandidateFeatures` (and any helpers it alone uses, e.g. matchesGameType if unreferenced elsewhere) from src/ai-trainer/feature-templates.ts:2446. Confirm the live feature-generator version covers the gameType filtering the templates copy intended; if it does not, that is a behavior gap to reconcile, not just a deletion.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed via Read/Grep. feature-templates.ts:2446 exports generateCandidateFeatures(structure) which filters on matchesGameType(template.requires.gameType, structure.winConditionInfo.gameType) and does no dedup. feature-generator.ts:7 exports an identically-named function that uses a seenIds Set for dedup and delegates filtering to templateApplies(). Grep confirms every consumer (benchmark-worker.ts:10/85, simulation-worker.ts:14/45, parallel-trainer.ts:20/110, parallel-simulator.test.ts) imports from './feature-generator.js', and index.ts:15 re-exports the generator version. The only imports from feature-templates.ts are FEATURE_TEMPLATES/FeatureTemplate (feature-generator.ts:2, index.ts:22). The feature-templates.ts copy of generateCandidateFeatures is therefore never imported or called — genuine dead code, and a divergent same-named duplicate. Citation and claim are correct. I downgrade from high to medium because the function is never executed (no runtime/behavioral impact); the harm is confined to the maintenance/single-source-of-truth hazard the finder describes (an edit to the dead copy silently no-ops).

findings.title: Lobby session callbacks (~85 lines) duplicated verbatim between GameSession.create and GameSession.restore
findings.evidence: src/session/game-session.ts:420 (inside `static create`, line 248) and src/session/game-session.ts:556 (inside `static restore`, line 533) both declare `const callbacks = { onGameStart() {...}, onAIConfigChanged(aiSlots) {...} }`. The onGameStart bodies are near-identical: both build `playerConfigs` from `storedState.lobbySlots`, recreate `GameRunner`, replace `session.#runner`/`#pickHandler`/`#pendingActionManager`, then sync player name/color from slots and call `#scheduleAICheck()`. jscpd flagged this as two clones totaling 85 lines (game-session.ts [418:21-450:23]≈[554:21-586:23] and [450:15-501:25]≈[586:37-637:25]). The only deltas are `gameType` vs `storedState.gameType` and `playerCount` vs `storedState.playerCount`.
findings.whyConcern: This is the core lobby-to-game handoff logic (player recreation, color/name sync, AI scheduling). A bug fix or new lobby field must be applied in two places; missing one silently breaks either fresh games or restored games depending on which path is exercised.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Game designers rely on lobby options (e.g. per-player isDictator, colors) surviving into the game. Divergence between create and restore means options can work on game start but be dropped after a reload, an invisible inconsistency.
findings.fixPaths: Extract a private static helper, e.g. `GameSession.#buildLobbyCallbacks({ GameClass, gameType, storedState, botAIConfig, getSession })` in src/session/game-session.ts and call it from both create (line ~420) and restore (line ~556). Parameterize the gameType/playerCount source so a single implementation serves both.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed against current source at /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/session/game-session.ts. The `const callbacks = {...}` object is declared twice: in `static create` at line 420 (onGameStart 421-488, onAIConfigChanged 489-508) and in `static restore` at line 556 (onGameStart 557-624, onAIConfigChanged 625-644). The onGameStart bodies are verbatim-identical: both build `playerConfigs` from `storedState.lobbySlots` (424-429 / 560-565), compute currentSlotCount/enginePlayerCount (432-433 / 568-569), construct `newGameOptions` + `newRunner`, then replace `session.#runner`, `session.#pickHandler = new PickHandler(...)`, `session.#pendingActionManager.updateRunner(...)` (455-462 / 591-598), sync player name/color from slots (467-482 / 603-618), and call `session.#scheduleAICheck()` (485-487 / 621-623). The only deltas are `gameType` (local) vs `storedState.gameType` in the GameRunner/AIController construction — functionally equivalent since create sets `storedState.gameType = gameType` at line 392. The onAIConfigChanged blocks are likewise near-identical. This is genuine ~85-line duplication of the core lobby-to-game handoff, and the divergence risk (a fix applied to one path but not the other) is real. I assign medium rather than high: the code works correctly today and the impact is latent maintainability/divergence risk, not an active bug — but it is structural duplication of meaningful, correctness-sensitive logic that warrants extraction.

findings.title: Game option metadata types duplicated across boardsmith/session and boardsmith/types (two sources of truth)
findings.evidence: src/session/types.ts:117 `NumberOption`, :131 `SelectOption`, :151 `BooleanOption`, :153 `GameOptionDefinition` are byte-for-byte identical to src/types/protocol.ts:104 `NumberOption`, :115 `SelectOption`, :127 `BooleanOption`, :134 `GameOptionDefinition`. Both are public: src/session/index.ts:59-60 re-exports `GameOptionDefinition`/`NumberOption`, and src/types/index.ts:11 `export * from './protocol.js'`. session/types.ts:4 even carries a header comment: 'Protocol types are also available from boardsmith/types for new code. See docs/consolidation.md' — but docs/consolidation.md does not exist (ls returned nothing). jscpd flagged 3 clones (~130 lines) between these two files.
findings.whyConcern: The same option-definition contract exists twice. If one copy gains a field (e.g. a new validation key on NumberOption) and the other does not, the two structurally diverge; TS will treat values typed against different copies as incompatible in nominal-ish positions, and consumers get inconsistent IntelliSense depending on the import path.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A game author importing `NumberOption`/`GameOptionDefinition` must guess between two equally-valid module specifiers (boardsmith/session vs boardsmith/types). The 'right path' is ambiguous, and the referenced migration doc is missing, so there is no guidance.
findings.fixPaths: Make src/types/protocol.ts the single owner of the option-metadata types and have src/session/types.ts `import type`/re-export them instead of redefining (it already imports LobbyState et al. from ../types/protocol.js at line 10). Either create the referenced docs/consolidation.md or drop the stale comment.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. src/session/types.ts:118 NumberOption, :131 SelectOption, :142 BooleanOption, :152 GameOptionDefinition are byte-for-byte identical in field bodies to src/types/protocol.ts:105/116/125/133. They are independent declarations, not re-exports: session/types.ts:10 imports only LobbyState/SlotStatus/LobbySlot/LobbyInfo from protocol, none of the option types. Both copies are public — src/session/index.ts re-exports GameOptionDefinition/NumberOption/SelectOption/BooleanOption, and src/types/index.ts:11 does `export * from './protocol.js'`. session/types.ts:4-5 points to docs/consolidation.md for migration, but `ls` confirms that file does not exist. So the duplication, dual public import paths, and dangling doc reference are all real. Severity medium (could be low): genuine two-sources-of-truth and pit-of-success ambiguity for game authors choosing between boardsmith/session and boardsmith/types, but impact is type-only with no runtime effect and divergence is currently hypothetical.

findings.title: saveAICode() is an exported no-op stub
findings.evidence: src/ai-trainer/code-generator.ts:387 `export function saveAICode(code: string, outputPath: string): void` has an empty body containing only comments: 'This would be called by the CLI / For now, just export the function / The actual file writing happens in the CLI command'. It takes a path and writes nothing. It is not re-exported from src/ai-trainer/index.ts (the code-generation block at index.ts:57-62 exports only generateAICode/updateAIWeights/types) and has zero callers in src, ~/BoardSmithGames, or ~/Dropbox/MERC.
findings.whyConcern: An exported function named saveAICode that silently does nothing violates fail-fast. If anything ever wires it up expecting a save, generated AI code is silently discarded with no error.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The name is a trap: an AI agent or developer scanning the module surface will reasonably call saveAICode(code, path) to persist output and get a silent no-op rather than a saved file or a clear error.
findings.fixPaths: Delete src/ai-trainer/code-generator.ts:387-394. If a save entry point is genuinely wanted, implement it with real fs writes (fail loudly on error) rather than leaving a stub.
findings.verifierReasoning: All three citations verified on current source. code-generator.ts:387-394 defines `export function saveAICode(code: string, outputPath: string): void` whose entire body is the three comments quoted in the finding ("This would be called by the CLI / For now, just export the function / The actual file writing happens in the CLI command") and writes nothing despite accepting an outputPath — a genuine no-op stub. index.ts:57-62 confirms the code-generation block re-exports only generateAICode, updateAIWeights, CodeGeneratorOptions, and UpdateWeightsOptions; saveAICode is not in the barrel. grep for "saveAICode" across /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src, ~/BoardSmithGames, and ~/Dropbox/MERC/BoardSmith/MERC returns only the definition line (code-generator.ts:387) — zero callers. So this is dead code and a fail-fast/pit-of-success trap (a function named to "save" that silently discards input). I downgrade severity from medium to low: because saveAICode is NOT re-exported from index.ts, it is only reachable by a direct deep import of code-generator.ts, which substantially limits the surface-scanning trap the finder describes; combined with zero callers, real-world risk is minor. Recommended action is simply deletion.

findings.title: runSimulationsWithProgress() is a dead, never-exported thin wrapper
findings.evidence: src/ai-trainer/simulator.ts:309 `export async function runSimulationsWithProgress<G>(GameClass, gameType, options, features)` simply returns `runSimulations(GameClass, gameType, { ...options, features })`. It is not in the simulator re-export block (src/ai-trainer/index.ts:24-32 lists runSimulations, simulateSingleGame, serialize/deserializeGameStructure but not this) and has no callers in src or the external game repos.
findings.whyConcern: Pure dead code that merely forwards to runSimulations after folding `features` into options — adds API surface and a maintenance target with no behavior.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: n/a — pure dead code.
findings.fixPaths: Delete src/ai-trainer/simulator.ts:309 and its function body.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. simulator.ts:309-319 defines `export async function runSimulationsWithProgress<G extends Game>(GameClass, gameType, options, features)` whose entire body is `return runSimulations(GameClass, gameType, { ...options, features })` — exactly the thin-wrapper-that-folds-features-into-options described. index.ts:24-32 re-exports only `runSimulations, simulateSingleGame, serializeGameStructure, deserializeGameStructure` plus the two types from simulator.js; `runSimulationsWithProgress` is NOT re-exported, so it is not part of the package public API (index.ts is the entry point), meaning external game repos cannot import it either. A grep for `runSimulationsWithProgress` across /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src and ~/BoardSmithGames returns only the definition site (simulator.ts:309) — zero callers. So it is genuine dead code: not consumed internally, not surfaced through the package entry, no callers anywhere searched. Low severity is correct — pure dead code, no behavior or pit-of-success impact, just unnecessary API surface/maintenance target.

findings.title: Orphaned dependency better-sqlite3 (and its @types) after server/SQLite-store removal
findings.evidence: package.json:78 declares `better-sqlite3` in dependencies and package.json:91 `@types/better-sqlite3` in devDependencies, but no source imports it: grep for better-sqlite3 / Database across src returns nothing, and no file references sqlite/SQLite. fallow reports it as an unused dependency. This aligns with MEMORY.md noting the server/worker modules (which held the SQLite game store) were deleted in F8/F9.
findings.whyConcern: better-sqlite3 is a native-compiled package; carrying it as a hard dependency forces every consumer of the boardsmith library to build/install a native module that nothing uses, slowing installs and risking build failures on platforms without build tooling.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Game authors installing boardsmith pay an install/compile cost for a feature that no longer exists, and may assume a SQLite store is available because the dep is present.
findings.fixPaths: Remove `better-sqlite3` (package.json:78) and `@types/better-sqlite3` (package.json:91) from package.json and update the lockfile.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified the citations on current source. package.json:78 declares "better-sqlite3": "^11.0.0" in dependencies, and package.json:91 declares "@types/better-sqlite3": "^7.6.0" in devDependencies — exactly as claimed. A repo-wide grep (excluding node_modules and dist) for `sqlite`, `better-sqlite3`, and `new Database(` returns zero matches across all .ts/.js/.mjs/.cjs files including src, bin, and scripts. So nothing in the codebase imports or references the package; it is a genuinely orphaned dependency, consistent with MEMORY.md noting the server/worker modules (which held the SQLite store) were deleted in F8/F9. The concern is real: better-sqlite3 is a native-compiled module, and because package.json ships `src` in its `files` array, library consumers must build/install this native dep for a feature that no longer exists. I concur with the finder's "low" severity — it is a legitimate dependency-hygiene/install-cost issue but not a functional or security defect; the fix is simply removing both entries.

findings.title: Session module imports its own barrel, creating circular dependencies
findings.evidence: fallow reports 2 cycles in session: src/session/index.ts -> src/session/snapshot-session-host.ts -> src/session/stateless-ops.ts, and src/session/index.ts -> src/session/stateless-ops.ts. Root cause: src/session/stateless-ops.ts:16 does `import { PickHandler, buildSingleActionMetadata, buildPlayerState, computeUndoInfo } from './index.js'` — i.e. it imports value symbols from the package's own barrel, which in turn re-exports stateless-ops. (A third cycle, src/engine/element/game.ts:107 importing checkForVolatileState from volatile-state.js while src/engine/element/volatile-state.ts:3 imports the value PersistentMap back from game.js, exists in engine.)
findings.whyConcern: Value-level import cycles through a barrel are an initialization-order hazard: depending on load order, one of the imported symbols can be `undefined` at module-evaluation time, producing intermittent 'X is not a function' failures that are hard to reproduce.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The easy path (import everything from ./index) is the wrong path here. Contributors naturally reach for the barrel, unknowingly tightening the cycle and risking init bugs.
findings.fixPaths: In src/session/stateless-ops.ts:16 import PickHandler/buildSingleActionMetadata/buildPlayerState/computeUndoInfo directly from their defining modules (e.g. ./pick-handler.js and the metadata/player-state source files) instead of ./index.js. For the engine cycle, move PersistentMap or checkForVolatileState into a shared leaf module so game.ts and volatile-state.ts no longer import each other.
findings.verifierReasoning: Citation confirmed on current source. src/session/stateless-ops.ts:16 does `import { PickHandler, buildSingleActionMetadata, buildPlayerState, computeUndoInfo } from './index.js'` — a value (non-type) import from the package's own barrel. src/session/index.ts re-exports `./stateless-ops.js` (line 137) and `./snapshot-session-host.js` (line 142), and snapshot-session-host.ts:1-2 imports from `./stateless-ops.js`, so both fallow-reported cycles genuinely exist. Crucially, the four imported symbols actually live in sibling modules — buildSingleActionMetadata/computeUndoInfo/buildPlayerState in src/session/utils.ts (lines 89/287/342) and PickHandler in src/session/pick-handler.ts:41 — so importing them from the barrel instead of directly is avoidable and is the sole cause of the cycle (note line 17 already imports buildActionTraces/computeElementDiff directly from './utils.js', making the barrel usage on line 16 inconsistent). Fix is trivial: import from './utils.js' and './pick-handler.js'. However, I downgrade medium->low because the finder's stated hazard ('intermittent X is not a function at module-eval') does not materialize here: every usage of these symbols is inside function bodies (stateless-ops.ts lines 130/227/256/292/320/345/497/520, all within functions defined at lines 126+), never at top-level module evaluation. ESM live bindings resolve such function-body references at call time, long after all modules finish evaluating, so there is no real initialization-order failure. It is a legitimate avoidable circular-dependency / pit-of-success cleanup, but low impact.

findings.title: Vestigial RNG call-count tracking in dev-state.ts is write-only dead code (always 0)
findings.evidence: src/engine/utils/dev-state.ts:83 declares `let randomCallCounter = 0;` whose ONLY incrementer is `createTrackedRandom` (dev-state.ts:89-94). `createTrackedRandom` has zero call sites anywhere (grep across src + BoardSmithGames + MERC returns only its definition). Therefore `randomCallCounter` is permanently 0. `getRandomCallCount` (dev-state.ts:99) and `resetRandomCallCounter` (dev-state.ts:106) likewise have zero callers. `captureDevState` writes `callCount: randomCallCounter` into `DevSnapshot.randomState` (dev-state.ts:137-146), and `randomState` is NEVER read by `restoreDevState` or by `GameSession.#reloadWithDevTransfer` (game-session.ts:949-1010 contains no `randomState`/`setRandomState` reference). The real RNG restore is done elsewhere via `setRandomState`/`getRandomState` (runner.ts, snapshot.ts, game.ts). The code even admits incompleteness at dev-state.ts:133-136: "We can't easily capture the exact random state... actual implementation will need GameSession to track this via a wrapper."
findings.whyConcern: An entire RNG-capture path was left behind after RNG restore moved to setRandomState/getRandomState. The counter can never be non-zero, `randomState.callCount` is written-but-never-read, and three exported functions are unreachable. Worse, after an HMR dev-transfer the RNG position is silently NOT restored through this path, so the snapshot's `randomState` is decorative — a latent correctness trap masked by code that looks like it handles RNG.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A maintainer reading captureDevState reasonably assumes RNG state is captured for HMR transfer; it is not. The dead `createTrackedRandom` wrapper invites someone to 'wire it in' rather than use the already-correct setRandomState path, steering future work down a dead abstraction.
findings.fixPaths: src/engine/utils/dev-state.ts: delete `randomCallCounter`, `createTrackedRandom`, `getRandomCallCount`, `resetRandomCallCounter`, and the `randomState` field from `DevSnapshot`/`captureDevState`; remove the corresponding re-exports in src/engine/utils/index.ts and src/engine/index.ts. If dev-transfer actually needs RNG continuity, capture `game.getRandomState()` and replay it via `setRandomState` in restoreDevState instead.
findings.verifierReasoning: All citations confirmed on current source. dev-state.ts:83 `randomCallCounter` is incremented only inside `createTrackedRandom` (dev-state.ts:89-94), which has zero call sites: grep across src/ shows it only defined and re-exported (engine/index.ts:230-232, engine/utils/index.ts:55-57), never invoked; grep across ~/BoardSmithGames and ~/Dropbox/MERC/BoardSmith/MERC returns zero usages of createTrackedRandom/getRandomCallCount/resetRandomCallCounter. Therefore randomCallCounter is permanently 0 and getRandomCallCount (:99) / resetRandomCallCounter (:106) are unreachable. captureDevState writes `callCount: randomCallCounter` into DevSnapshot.randomState (dev-state.ts:137-140), but restoreDevState (:165-231) never reads snapshot.randomState — it constructs `new GameClass(options.gameOptions)` with no RNG fast-forward. game-session.ts #reloadWithDevTransfer (:949-1013) calls captureDevState/restoreDevState (lines 951, 1004) and contains no randomState/setRandomState reference (confirmed by grep + read). The one external hit, MERC mcts-clone.test.ts:127-128 `clone.setRandomState(snapshot.randomState)`, operates on a different snapshot type carrying a real RNG-state object, corroborating that the live RNG path is setRandomState/getRandomState, not this DevSnapshot field. The honest incompleteness comment exists at dev-state.ts:133-136. CLAUDE.md's external-API false-positive caveat does not apply since no external game consumes these exports. I lowered severity from medium to low: the dead code and misleading randomState field are real, but the only correctness implication (RNG position not restored) is confined to dev-time HMR; production/AI determinism uses the separate, correct setRandomState/getRandomState path. This is cleanup-grade dead code, not a shipping-impact bug.

findings.title: Replay-file module (replay.ts) is fully unused exported dead code
findings.evidence: src/engine/utils/replay.ts defines `ReplayFile`, `createReplayFile`, `validateReplayFile`, `parseReplayFile`. These are re-exported from src/engine/index.ts (lines 22-26) and src/runtime/index.ts (createReplayFile/validateReplayFile/parseReplayFile/ReplayFile block). Grep across all of src (including *.test.ts), BoardSmithGames, and Dropbox/MERC/BoardSmith/MERC finds zero references outside replay.ts itself and the two index re-export lines. The actual replay/restore path is snapshot-based (`GameRunner.fromSnapshot`), and runner.ts:284-305 documents that replay-based restore was removed as unsound.
findings.whyConcern: 102 lines plus two index re-exports of public API that nothing calls. The whole 'replay file' concept is orphaned after the move to state-authoritative snapshots.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Exporting `createReplayFile`/`parseReplayFile` advertises a supported 'save/replay a game from an action log' feature that is not wired into anything and conflicts with the snapshot-based design — a game author who builds on it will hit the exact unsound-replay class of bug runner.ts warns about.
findings.fixPaths: Delete src/engine/utils/replay.ts and remove its re-export blocks from src/engine/index.ts and src/runtime/index.ts. If a portable replay format is still desired, derive it from GameStateSnapshot.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. src/engine/utils/replay.ts (lines 1-102) defines ReplayFile, createReplayFile, validateReplayFile, parseReplayFile as claimed. Grep across all of src (incl. *.test.ts) shows references ONLY in replay.ts and three re-export index files: src/runtime/index.ts:23-26, src/engine/index.ts:217-219/244, and src/engine/utils/index.ts:31-37 — no actual call sites. Grep across ~/BoardSmithGames and ~/Dropbox/MERC/BoardSmith/MERC returns zero hits, so it is not consumed by any external game (the standard false-positive case does not apply). runner.ts:284-305 documents that replay-based restore was removed as unsound in favor of state-authoritative fromSnapshot, corroborating the orphaned-concept framing. Minor citation errors that do not affect the verdict: there are three re-export sites not two (finder missed engine/utils/index.ts), and engine line numbers are 217-219/244 not 22-26 — both understate the dead surface. Low severity is appropriate: genuinely unused public API with no current correctness impact, but it advertises an unsound action-log replay capability conflicting with the snapshot design.

findings.title: Two dead private helpers in action.ts (choicesContain, getChoiceFilterValue)
findings.evidence: src/engine/action/action.ts:457 `private choicesContain(...)` has zero `this.choicesContain` call sites in the file (only `annotatedChoicesContain` is used). src/engine/action/action.ts:1013 `private getChoiceFilterValue(...)` has zero `this.getChoiceFilterValue` call sites and no references anywhere in src (grep returns only the definition).
findings.whyConcern: Pure dead private methods left after the choice-matching code converged on `annotatedChoicesContain`/`valuesEqual` and on `ChoiceSelection.filterBy.selectionName`. They add surface to an already 1600-line file.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: n/a — pure dead code.
findings.fixPaths: src/engine/action/action.ts: remove the `choicesContain` (line ~457) and `getChoiceFilterValue` (line ~1013) method bodies and their doc comments.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified against current source. grep across src/ shows `choicesContain` appears only at its definition (src/engine/action/action.ts:457) with zero `this.choicesContain` call sites — the actually-used helper is the sibling `annotatedChoicesContain` (defined 464, called at 633, 646, 1304). `getChoiceFilterValue` (src/engine/action/action.ts:1013) appears only at its definition with no other reference anywhere in src/. Both are `private` methods (read confirms `private choicesContain(...)` at 457 and `private getChoiceFilterValue(...)` at 1013), so the "used by external game" false-positive escape does not apply — private members cannot be consumed externally. Both are genuine pure dead code. Severity low is appropriate: no runtime/correctness/security impact, just unused surface in a large file.

findings.title: Cluster of exported designer helpers with zero consumers (selection filters + arg resolvers + namedSequence)
findings.evidence: Verified zero call sites (grep `name(` across src incl tests, BoardSmithGames, MERC, excluding definitions/docs): `allOf`, `anyOf`, `adjacentToSelection`, `excludeAlreadySelected` (all defined in src/engine/action/helpers.ts), `resolveElementArg`, `isResolvedElement` (src/engine/utils/action-helpers.ts — the entire file), and `namedSequence` (src/engine/flow/builders.ts:39). Each is exported through its module sub-index and src/engine/index.ts but is never called internally, in any test, or in any of the example games (hex, checkers, go-fish, cribbage, polyhedral-potions, demos) or MERC.
findings.whyConcern: These are documented, advertised public helpers (filter combinators, followUp arg resolution, named flow steps) that no shipping game or internal code path exercises. Untested, unused API drifts out of sync with the code it is meant to wrap (e.g. resolveElementArg's contract depends on the server's arg-resolution behavior, which has no test pinning it).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A large unused, untested helper surface dilutes the 'obvious right path': a game author scanning the API can't tell which helpers are load-bearing vs. abandoned, and may build on `resolveElementArg`/`allOf` that have no regression coverage.
findings.fixPaths: Either (a) delete the unused exports — src/engine/action/helpers.ts (allOf/anyOf/adjacentToSelection/excludeAlreadySelected), src/engine/utils/action-helpers.ts (whole file), src/engine/flow/builders.ts (namedSequence) — plus their re-exports in src/engine/action/index.ts, src/engine/utils/index.ts, src/engine/flow/index.ts, src/engine/index.ts; or (b) if they are intended public API, add at least one example-game usage and a unit test per helper to keep them honest.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all citations on current source. Definitions confirmed: allOf (src/engine/action/helpers.ts:327), anyOf (:347), adjacentToSelection (:262), excludeAlreadySelected (:301); resolveElementArg (src/engine/utils/action-helpers.ts:28) and isResolvedElement (:53) — the entire file; namedSequence (src/engine/flow/builders.ts:39). findings.title: CLI flags accepted but silently ignored: `validate --fix`, `validate --skip-simulation`, `lint --fix`
findings.evidence: src/cli/cli.ts:67-68 registers `--fix` and `--skip-simulation` on the validate command; src/cli/cli.ts:75 registers `--fix` on lint. But validate.ts:20 `validateCommand(options)` never reads `options` anywhere in the function body (only reference to `options` is the parameter declaration — confirmed by `grep -n options src/cli/commands/validate.ts` returning only line 20). lint.ts:263 `lintCommand(options)` likewise never reads `options` (grep returns only line 263; `grep -n 'options\.' src/cli/commands/lint.ts` returns nothing). validateCommand additionally runs no simulation at all (validateMetadata/TypeScript/Security/AssetPaths/BundleSize/RequiredFiles only), so `--skip-simulation` controls behavior that does not exist.
findings.whyConcern: Three user-facing flags are parsed and then discarded. `boardsmith validate --fix` claims it will 'Attempt to auto-fix issues' and exits with the same failures unfixed. `boardsmith validate --skip-simulation` implies a random-simulation step exists that can be skipped — it does not. A user trusting these flags gets a silent no-op, the opposite of fail-fast/loud.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Directly violates the Pit of Success: the help text advertises capabilities the code does not implement, luring a game designer into believing auto-fix ran or a slow simulation was skipped. The wrong path (relying on the flag) looks like the right path.
findings.fixPaths: Either implement the flags or remove them. Cleanest: delete the three `.option(...)` lines in src/cli/cli.ts (67,68,75) and the unused `fix`/`skipSimulation` fields in ValidateOptions (validate.ts:8-11) and LintOptions (lint.ts). If auto-fix is genuinely planned, gate it behind a clear 'not yet implemented' error rather than a silent no-op.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Citations check out on current source. cli.ts:67-68 registers `--fix` and `--skip-simulation` on the validate command, and cli.ts:75 registers `--fix` on lint (text: "Attempt to auto-fix issues (coming soon)"). validate.ts:20 declares `validateCommand(options: ValidateOptions)` but `grep -c options validate.ts` returns 1 — the only occurrence is the parameter on line 20; the body (lines 21-89) never reads `options`. The body runs exactly six checks (validateMetadata, validateTypeScript, validateSecurity, validateAssetPaths, validateBundleSize, validateRequiredFiles) and contains no simulation step at all — `grep -ni simulat validate.ts` matches only the `skipSimulation?` field on the interface (line 10), confirming `--skip-simulation` gates behavior that does not exist. lint.ts:263 declares `lintCommand(options: LintOptions)` and `grep -n 'options\.' lint.ts` returns nothing, so its `--fix` is also discarded. So all three flags are parsed and silently ignored — the finding is accurate.

I downgrade severity from medium to low. The impact is real but bounded: validate still prints and exits non-zero on actual failures, so `--fix` cannot trick a user into thinking validation passed (the failures remain visible) — it just fails to auto-fix. lint's `--fix` help text already says "(coming soon)", which is honest signposting, weakening that sub-claim. `--skip-simulation` is a pure no-op with no slow step to skip. No data corruption, security, or correctness consequence — this is a CLI honesty/dead-flag UX issue, hence low rather than medium. It does genuinely violate fail-loud / Pit-of-Success and is worth fixing (either implement or remove the flags, and drop the unused `skipSimulation` field).

findings.title: Orphaned ParallelTrainer training subtree — exported and documented as the primary training API, but no command or consumer drives it
findings.evidence: src/ai-trainer/index.ts:2 exports `ParallelTrainer`. The only reference to the class anywhere in src/, all 8 example games, and the MERC game is its own definition file (`grep ParallelTrainer` outside index.ts/docs matches only src/ai-trainer/parallel-trainer.ts). No `train-ai` CLI command exists (grep for train-ai/'train' in src/cli is empty); the only AI command is `evolve-ai-weights` (cli.ts:88) which uses `WeightEvolver` (evolve-ai-weights.ts:109,142), not ParallelTrainer. parallel-trainer.ts is 565 lines. Its dependency `runParallelSimulations` (parallel-simulator.ts → spawns simulation-worker.ts) has no non-test consumer except parallel-trainer.ts; `runSimulations`/`simulateSingleGame` (simulator.ts) are reachable only via that same dead chain (benchmark-worker.ts imports only deserializeGameStructure, not the simulation runners). The live evolve path is weight-evolver → parallel-benchmark → benchmark-worker. Meanwhile docs/api/ai-trainer.md:220-223 instructs users `import { ParallelTrainer } from 'boardsmith/ai-trainer'` as the training entry point.
findings.whyConcern: ~565 lines of trainer plus parallel-simulator.ts and simulation-worker.ts (and runSimulations/simulateSingleGame in simulator.ts) are dead: nothing in the repo, example games, or MERC instantiates ParallelTrainer or calls the parallel/serial simulation runners. The phase docs show a `train-ai` command was removed but the class it powered was left behind.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: docs/api/ai-trainer.md points game developers at ParallelTrainer as the way to train AI, but the supported/maintained path is the `evolve-ai-weights` command (WeightEvolver). A developer following the docs adopts an unexercised code path instead of the real one — a textbook wrong-path-looks-right trap.
findings.fixPaths: Remove parallel-trainer.ts, parallel-simulator.ts, simulation-worker.ts, and the unused runSimulations/simulateSingleGame in simulator.ts (keep serializeGameStructure/deserializeGameStructure used by the benchmark workers); drop their exports from src/ai-trainer/index.ts; and update docs/api/ai-trainer.md to document WeightEvolver/evolve-ai-weights as the training API. Verify `evolve-ai-weights` still runs end-to-end afterward.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified every cited claim against current source:

1. Export confirmed: src/ai-trainer/index.ts:2 `export { ParallelTrainer, type ParallelTrainingConfig } from './parallel-trainer.js'`.
2. Orphaned class confirmed: `grep -rn ParallelTrainer src/` excluding index.ts and parallel-trainer.ts returns EMPTY. No consumer in src.
3. No external consumer: grep for ParallelTrainer across all 8 example games (~/BoardSmithGames: checkers, cribbage, demo-action-panel, demo-animation, demo-complex-ui, go-fish, hex, polyhedral-potions) and MERC (~/Dropbox/MERC/BoardSmith/MERC) returns EMPTY (directories confirmed to exist, so the empty result is meaningful, not a bad path).
4. No train-ai CLI command: grep for train-ai/'train' in src/cli is empty. The only AI command is `evolve-ai-weights` (cli.ts:88).
5. Live path uses WeightEvolver, not ParallelTrainer: evolve-ai-weights.ts:109,142 instantiates `WeightEvolver`; no ParallelTrainer reference.
6. Dead dependency chain confirmed: `runParallelSimulations` (parallel-simulator.ts:59) is imported only by parallel-trainer.ts:21 (plus the index.ts re-export). `simulateSingleGame`/`runSimulations` (simulator.ts) are reachable only via simulation-worker.ts and internal simulator calls — that chain hangs off the dead ParallelTrainer path.
7. Live benchmark path confirmed separate: benchmark-worker.ts imports only `deserializeGameStructure` from simulator.js (line 9), NOT the simulation runners — grep for simulateSingleGame/runSimulations in benchmark-worker.ts is empty.
8. Docs trap confirmed: docs/api/ai-trainer.md (~line 219-223) instructs `import { ParallelTrainer } from 'boardsmith/ai-trainer'` under a "Parallel Training" heading as the training entry point.

File size confirmed: parallel-trainer.ts is 565 lines. The finding accurately describes a dead training subtree (ParallelTrainer + parallel-simulator + simulation-worker + the runSimulations/simulateSingleGame branch of simulator.ts) that the docs actively recommend as the way to train AI, while the supported path is the WeightEvolver-based evolve-ai-weights command.

Severity: medium is appropriate. This is more than inert dead code — it is a pit-of-success violation: docs point game developers at an unexercised code path (ParallelTrainer) instead of the maintained one (evolve-ai-weights/WeightEvolver). Not critical/high because nothing functionally breaks and the symbols are library public-API exports, but the documented-yet-dead trap plus ~565+ lines of unreachable trainer/simulator code justifies medium.

dimensions.summary: The Docs-vs-Code dimension is the weakest area surfaced by the audit: documentation has drifted substantially from the actual source API, with five high-severity cases where docs describe interfaces that simply do not exist — a fabricated `game.players` collection (`.get()`, `.current`, `.all()`, `.nextAfter()`), a fabricated GameConnection API in client.md (~12 nonexistent methods), a custom-Player constructor signature the base class lacks, ui-components.md's `actionController.error`/`.pendingAction` (real names: `lastError`/`currentAction`), and an ai-system.md GameSession example invoking a private constructor, wrong option names, and a nonexistent `start()`. Medium-severity drift compounds this across runtime.md (GameRunner's `executeAction`/`getActionHistory`/`getStateAt` instead of the real positional-arg `performAction`), dice-and-scoring.md (UniqueTrack `pointsPerEntry` shown as a function but typed as `number[]`), composable return members (`getAlgebraic`/`pixelToCell`, `hexToPixel`/`pixelToHex`), and architecture.md still documenting the deleted `server` and `worker` modules. A scatter of low-severity naming mismatches (ClaimPositionRequest vs. ClaimSeatRequest, importing `Game` from `boardsmith/ai`) rounds out the pattern. The encouraging signal is that nearly every finding is a documentation-accuracy fix rather than a code defect — the underlying APIs are real and coherent, so the corrective work is mechanical reconciliation against source rather than redesign; still, the breadth and the number of high-severity fabrications mean the docs are currently a liability for new developers and should be treated as a blocking cleanup, ideally backed by doc-example compilation tests to prevent recurrence.

findings.title: Docs use a non-existent `game.players` collection API (`.get()`, `.current`, `.all()`, `.nextAfter()`)
findings.evidence: `game.players` is a plain array getter, not a collection: engine/element/game.ts:1478 `get players(): P[] { return (this._t.children...).filter(...).sort(...) }`. The real accessors are methods on Game: `getPlayer(seat)` (engine/element/game.ts:1569), `currentPlayer` getter (engine/element/game.ts:1512), `nextAfter(player)` (engine/element/game.ts:1765). There is no PlayerCollection class anywhere (`grep -rn 'class PlayerCollection' engine/` returns nothing). The real Go Fish game confirms the correct API: ~/BoardSmithGames/go-fish/src/rules/actions.ts:39 `game.getPlayer(choice.value)`. Yet the docs call `game.players.get(...)`, `.current`, `.all()`, `.nextAfter()`: core-concepts.md:236-244 (`this.players.get(1)`, `this.players.current`, `this.players.all()`, `ctx.game.players.current`); actions-and-flow.md:298, 689, 708, 917 (`game.players.get(choice.value)`, `ctx.game.players.get(1)`) -- 689/708 are explicitly labeled 'From Go Fish actions.ts' but the real file uses `game.getPlayer`; common-patterns.md:21,28,35 (`this.players.get(...)`, `this.players.nextAfter(...)`); common-pitfalls.md:861 (`testGame.game.players.get(1)`); nomenclature.md:135 (`game.players.current`).
findings.whyConcern: `game.players` returns a plain `Array`, so `.get()`, `.current`, `.all()`, and `.nextAfter()` are all `undefined` -- calling them throws `TypeError: ... is not a function` (or yields `undefined` for `.current`). Every doc-copied snippet that looks up a player by seat or gets the current player crashes at runtime and fails TypeScript compilation. This is the single most pervasive copy-paste seed in the docs, spanning five of the seven audited files.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: This is the canonical pattern a game designer or AI agent copies first when they need 'the player at seat N' or 'the current player'. The easy path the docs teach is broken; the correct path (`game.getPlayer(n)`, `game.currentPlayer`, `game.nextAfter(p)`) is never shown. Worse, the docs falsely attribute the broken form to the working Go Fish example, so a reader who cross-checks is doubly misled.
findings.fixPaths: Global replace across core-concepts.md, actions-and-flow.md, common-patterns.md, common-pitfalls.md, nomenclature.md: `game.players.get(x)`->`game.getPlayer(x)`, `game.players.current`->`game.currentPlayer`, `game.players.all()`->`game.players` (already an array), `this.players.nextAfter(p)`->`this.nextAfter(p)`. Verify each against engine/element/game.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified against current source (paths are under src/, e.g. src/engine/element/game.ts, but content matches citations exactly). src/engine/element/game.ts:1478 `get players(): P[]` returns a plain filtered/sorted array — not a collection. The real accessors are Game members: getPlayer(seat) at :1569, currentPlayer getter at :1512, nextAfter(player) at :1765 (plus getPlayerOrThrow at :1604). `grep -rn 'PlayerCollection' src/` returns nothing, confirming no collection class. Because `players` is a JS Array, `.get()`, `.all()`, and `.nextAfter()` are not methods (runtime TypeError + TS compile error) and `.current` is undefined. Confirmed every cited doc usage: core-concepts.md:237-244 (`.get(1)`, `.get(2)`, `.current`, `.all()`, `ctx.game.players.current`), actions-and-flow.md:298/689/708/917, common-patterns.md:21/28/35, common-pitfalls.md:861, nomenclature.md:135 — plus an additional uncited instance at ai-system.md:101 (`g.players.get(p)`). Confirmed the false attribution: actions-and-flow.md:689/708 are labeled 'From Go Fish actions.ts' but the real ~/BoardSmithGames/go-fish/src/rules/actions.ts:39 and :84 use `game.getPlayer(choice.value)`. Severity high (not critical) because the defect is documentation-only — no shipping code uses the broken form — but it is the canonical, first-copied player-lookup pattern, spans 6 doc files, never shows the correct API, and is falsely credited to a working example, making it the most pervasive copy-paste seed in the docs.

findings.title: core-concepts.md 'PlayerCollection Serialization Warning' describes a class and toJSON quirk that do not exist
findings.evidence: core-concepts.md:280-297 has a whole section asserting `game.players` is a `PlayerCollection` (an Array subclass) whose `.map()` returns a PlayerCollection and whose `PlayerCollection.toJSON()` 're-serializes the elements, losing any custom properties', prescribing `[...game.players.map(...)]` / `Array.from(...)` as the fix. But `game.players` is a plain array (engine/element/game.ts:1478 `get players(): P[]`), and no `PlayerCollection` class exists in the codebase (`grep -rn 'class PlayerCollection' engine/` -> no matches; `grep -rn 'PlayerCollection' engine/` -> no matches). core-concepts.md:236 also annotates `this.players // PlayerCollection`. (The same fabricated warning is duplicated in the out-of-scope llm-overview.md:422-434.)
findings.whyConcern: The documented failure mode (custom properties lost when mapping over players because a subclass toJSON re-runs) cannot occur, because the value is a plain Array with no custom toJSON. Readers will add pointless `[...]`/`Array.from()` defensive wrappers cargo-culted from a non-existent quirk, and -- more damaging -- will trust a mental model of player serialization that is wrong, making real serialization bugs harder to reason about.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Teaches a false invariant about a core data structure. A designer debugging a genuine serialization issue will chase the phantom 'PlayerCollection.toJSON re-serialization' instead of the real cause, and will distrust straightforward `game.players.map(...)` code that is actually correct.
findings.fixPaths: Delete or rewrite core-concepts.md:280-297 (and the annotation at :236). If a real serialization caveat exists, document the actual `Player.toJSON()` behavior from engine/player/player.ts; otherwise remove the section.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed all citations. core-concepts.md:280-297 has a "PlayerCollection Serialization Warning" asserting game.players is a PlayerCollection (Array subclass) with a toJSON() re-serialization quirk, prescribing [...]/Array.from() fixes; line 236 also annotates this.players // PlayerCollection. The real source at src/engine/element/game.ts:1478 is `get players(): P[]` returning a plain array via filter().sort(), so .map() yields a plain Array and the documented quirk cannot occur. No PlayerCollection class exists outside .planning docs; it was deleted in phase 12 (.planning/phases/12-player-in-tree/12-01-SUMMARY.md:7). The same fabricated warning is duplicated in llm-overview.md:422-434. The docs document a non-existent class and a false serialization invariant, and their "WRONG" example is actually correct while the "CORRECT" fix adds pointless wrappers. Severity medium rather than high: no runtime bug results and the wrappers are harmless, but it propagates a wrong mental model of a core data structure and will misdirect real serialization debugging.

findings.title: core-concepts.md custom Player example uses a constructor signature the base class does not have
findings.evidence: core-concepts.md:206-221 shows `export class MyPlayer extends Player<MyGame, MyPlayer> { ... constructor(position: number, name: string, game: MyGame) { super(position, name); this.game = game; this.hand = game.create(Hand, ...); } }`. The actual base Player constructor takes a single element-context arg: engine/player/player.ts:127 `constructor(ctx: Partial<ElementContext>) { super(ctx); }`. There is no `(position, name)` overload, so `super(position, name)` is a compile error. Real custom Player classes confirm the pattern: they declare extra fields and define NO constructor at all -- ~/BoardSmithGames/checkers/src/rules/elements.ts:119 `export class CheckersPlayer extends Player { capturedCount = 0; }` and ~/BoardSmithGames/go-fish/src/rules/elements.ts:40 `export class GoFishPlayer extends Player { bookCount = 0; }`.
findings.whyConcern: The snippet does not compile (`super(position, name)` mismatches `Partial<ElementContext>`) and teaches creating per-player hands inside the Player constructor, which is not how players are instantiated by the engine -- players are created by the framework from `playerCount`, not hand-constructed with positional args. A reader following this will fight type errors and an incorrect lifecycle model.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: This is the first thing a designer copies to add custom player state (score, hand). The shown path (override constructor, call `super(position,name)`, create hand in constructor) is wrong; the correct path (just declare fields, optionally initialize hands in the Game constructor as common-patterns.md:254-264 actually does) is contradicted within the same doc set.
findings.fixPaths: Rewrite core-concepts.md:206-221 to match real usage: declare extra fields with initializers and no constructor; move hand creation into the Game constructor loop over `this.players` (mirror common-patterns.md:254-264 and the checkers/go-fish elements.ts).
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. core-concepts.md:206-221 shows `MyPlayer extends Player<MyGame,MyPlayer>` with `constructor(position: number, name: string, game: MyGame) { super(position, name); ... this.hand = game.create(Hand, ...) }`. The base Player has a single constructor at src/engine/player/player.ts:127 `constructor(ctx: Partial<ElementContext>) { super(ctx); }` — no `(position, name)` overload, so `super(position, name)` is a compile error. The correct pattern is verified by real games which declare extra fields and define NO constructor: checkers/src/rules/elements.ts:119 `CheckersPlayer extends Player { capturedCount = 0 }` and go-fish/src/rules/elements.ts:40 `GoFishPlayer extends Player { bookCount = 0 }`. The snippet does not compile and teaches an incorrect player-instantiation/lifecycle model (players are framework-created from playerCount, not hand-constructed with positional args). Because this is the primary copy-paste example for adding custom player state in a core concepts doc, the pit-of-success impact is significant — high severity.

findings.title: architecture.md documents `server` and `worker` modules (and their exports) that have been deleted from the codebase

findings.title: ui-components.md documents actionController.error and .pendingAction — neither property exists (real names: lastError, currentAction)
findings.evidence: docs/ui-components.md:1361 documents controller state `pendingAction | string | null` and :1366 `error | string | null | Error message from last failed execution`. The whole 'Error Handling in UI' section relies on these: :1453 `v-if="actionController.error"`, :1469, :1486 `watch(() => props.actionController.error, ...)`, :1523/:1534, :1556-1594 read `actionController.error`; :1443 `if (actionController.pendingAction === 'move')`. The real public API has NO such properties. Source src/ui/composables/useActionControllerTypes.ts:288 declares `currentAction: Ref<string | null>` and :304 `lastError: Ref<string | null>` (no `error`, no `pendingAction`). The controller's returned object confirms it: src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:1443 returns `currentAction`, :1449 returns `lastError` — there is no `error` or `pendingAction` key (full return block :1441-1487). The sibling doc custom-ui-guide.md:651 and :794 correctly use `actionController.lastError.value`.
findings.whyConcern: A designer copying the prominent 'Action Controller API' and 'Error Handling in UI' reference sections writes `actionController.error` and `actionController.pendingAction`, both of which are `undefined` at runtime. Error banners/toasts never render (the bound value is always falsy), and `pendingAction === 'move'` branches are dead. Worse, these are refs — even with the correct name they require `.value` in script and template (nested controller props are not auto-unwrapped), yet every example omits `.value`.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The most authoritative UI reference doc steers users to non-existent property names, so the easy path silently does nothing instead of failing loudly. Error handling — the one place you most want fail-loud — is exactly what breaks invisibly.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ui-components.md: rename `pendingAction`→`currentAction` and `error`→`lastError` in the state table (:1359-1367) and throughout the Error Handling section (:1453-1594), and add `.value` to every access (e.g. `actionController.lastError.value`, `actionController.currentAction.value`). Match the already-correct usage in custom-ui-guide.md.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed against current source. docs/ui-components.md:1362 documents controller state `pendingAction` and :1366 documents `error`; these names drive the prominent "Reading controller state" (:1443 `actionController.pendingAction === 'move'`, :1453 `v-if="actionController.error"`) and entire "Error Handling in UI" section (:1469 etc). The real public API has neither: useActionControllerTypes.ts:288 declares `currentAction: Ref<string|null>` and :304 `lastError: Ref<string|null>` (no `error`, no `pendingAction`). The controller's returned object (useActionController.ts:1441-1487) lists `currentAction` and `lastError` and contains no `error` or `pendingAction` key. The sibling doc custom-ui-guide.md:651/:794 correctly uses `actionController.lastError.value`, corroborating both the real name and the required `.value` unwrap that ui-components.md omits. So the most authoritative UI reference points users to undefined properties, making error banners always falsy and `pendingAction` branches dead — error handling, the place you most want fail-loud, breaks invisibly. Docs-only (no runtime code broken), so high rather than critical, but high is warranted given it is the primary UI reference and the breakage is silent.

findings.title: ui-components.md actionController method table has wrong signatures (skip arg, async return types)
findings.evidence: docs/ui-components.md:1343-1347 documents: `execute | (name, args?) => Promise<void>`, `start | (name, options?) => void`, `fill | (name, value) => void`, `skip | () => void`. Source disagrees on all four. src/ui/composables/useActionControllerTypes.ts:332 `execute: (...) => Promise<ActionResult>` (not `Promise<void>`); :352-355 `start: (...) => Promise<void>` (not `void`); :356-357 `fill: (selectionName, value) => Promise<ValidationResult>` (async, not `void`); :358-359 `skip: (selectionName: string) => void` — skip REQUIRES the selection name. Implementation src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:1332 `function skip(selectionName: string)` then :1335 `selections.find(s => s.name === selectionName)` — calling `skip()` with no argument (as shown at ui-components.md:1437 `actionController.skip();`) finds nothing and no-ops.
findings.whyConcern: `skip()` with no argument silently does nothing, so a designer's 'Skip' button appears broken with no error. Documenting `execute` as `Promise<void>` hides the `ActionResult` (with `.success`/`.error`) that custom-ui-guide.md:129 correctly relies on, and documenting `fill`/`start` as `void` hides that they are awaitable async — leading to missed `await` and race conditions during followUp chains.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The signature table is the quick-reference designers trust; the wrong-arg `skip()` and the hidden return values lure them into code that compiles in JS but behaves wrongly at runtime.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ui-components.md:1343-1347 — correct to `execute(name, args?) => Promise<ActionResult>`, `start(name, options?) => Promise<void>`, `fill(name, value) => Promise<ValidationResult>`, `skip(selectionName) => void`; update the `actionController.skip();` example at :1437 to pass the selection name.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified all four signature mismatches in the `Controller Methods` table at docs/ui-components.md:1343-1346 against the canonical type in src/ui/composables/useActionControllerTypes.ts:

1. `execute`: doc says `(name, args?) => Promise<void>` (1343); source line 332 is `execute: (actionName, args?) => Promise<ActionResult>`. Mismatch confirmed. The ActionResult is real and relied upon — custom-ui-guide.md:129-132 does `const result = await actionController.execute(...); if (!result.success) console.error(result.error)`, which the `Promise<void>` doc hides.

2. `start`: doc says `=> void` (1344); source 352-355 is `=> Promise<void>` (async, fetches choices). Mismatch confirmed.

3. `fill`: doc says `=> void` (1345); source 356-357 is `fill: (selectionName, value) => Promise<ValidationResult>` (async). Mismatch confirmed.

4. `skip`: doc says `() => void` with no arg (1346); source 358-359 is `skip: (selectionName: string) => void`. The implementation at useActionController.ts:1332 `function skip(selectionName: string)` then 1335 `currentActionMeta.value.selections.find(s => s.name === selectionName)` and 1336 `if (!selection?.optional) return;` — calling `skip()` with no argument resolves to `find(s => s.name === undefined)` → undefined → silent no-op. The doc's own usage example at ui-components.md:1434 shows exactly the buggy call `actionController.skip();` with no argument.

The finding is accurate on every cited line. This is a genuine docs-mismatch: the quick-reference table designers rely on understates async return types (risking missed `await`/races) and, most concretely, documents `skip()` as taking no argument while it requires the selection name — propagated into a wrong code example that silently does nothing. Severity medium rather than high because it is documentation-only (no production code defect) and TypeScript users would get a compile error from the real typed `skip`, limiting blast radius to JS/loosely-typed custom UIs and copy-paste of the example.

findings.title: useGameGrid documented return members getAlgebraic/pixelToCell do not exist
findings.evidence: docs/ui-components.md:962 shows `const { getCellAt, getAlgebraic, pixelToCell } = useGameGrid({ rows: 8, cols: 8, cellSize: 60 });`. Source src/ui/composables/useGameGrid.ts:209-221 returns `{ board, grid, getKey, getCellAt, getChildAt, getChildrenAt, toNotation, fromNotation, iterateCells, findCells, isInBounds }` — there is no `getAlgebraic` and no `pixelToCell` anywhere in the file (grep confirms only `toNotation` :164 and `fromNotation` :173 exist as notation helpers). `getCellAt` is the only documented member that is real.
findings.whyConcern: Destructuring `getAlgebraic` and `pixelToCell` yields `undefined`; calling them throws `TypeError: x is not a function`. The actual API for notation is `toNotation`/`fromNotation`, which the doc never mentions for the composable.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A grid-game author follows the reference and immediately hits a runtime crash with no pointer to the correct method names.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ui-components.md:961-970 — replace `getAlgebraic`/`pixelToCell` with the real return members (`toNotation`, `fromNotation`, `getCellAt`, etc.) from useGameGrid.ts:209-221.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. docs/ui-components.md:962 destructures `const { getCellAt, getAlgebraic, pixelToCell } = useGameGrid(...)`. The composable in src/ui/composables/useGameGrid.ts returns exactly `{ board, grid, getKey, getCellAt, getChildAt, getChildrenAt, toNotation, fromNotation, iterateCells, findCells, isInBounds }` (lines 209-221). Grep for `getAlgebraic` and `pixelToCell` across the file returns no matches (exit 1), so neither member exists. Only `getCellAt` of the three documented members is real; the notation helpers are actually `toNotation` (164) / `fromNotation` (173), which the doc snippet never mentions for the composable (it instead uses the standalone `toAlgebraicNotation`/`fromAlgebraicNotation` imports at line 960/969-970). So the destructured `getAlgebraic`/`pixelToCell` resolve to `undefined`, and any author following the example to call them hits a TypeError. The finding's citations and reasoning are accurate. I keep medium rather than higher because the shown snippet does not itself invoke the two missing members (it relies on the standalone functions), so it is a misleading docs-mismatch that breaks on use rather than an immediate copy-paste crash; still a genuine Pit-of-Success failure that points authors at nonexistent API.

findings.title: useHexGrid documented return members (hexToPixel, pixelToHex, getHexCorners) are not on the composable's return
findings.evidence: docs/ui-components.md:985-991 shows `const { hexToPixel, pixelToHex, getHexCorners, getNeighbors } = useHexGrid({ size: 30, orientation: 'flat' });`. The composable's return type src/ui/composables/useHexGrid.ts:85-119 exposes `getHexPosition` (:104), `getHexPoints` (:106), `getNeighbors` (:116), `hexDistance` (:114), `axialToCube`/`cubeToAxial`, etc. There is no `pixelToHex` and no `getHexCorners` in the file at all; `hexToPixel` exists ONLY as a standalone export (:375), not as a member returned by `useHexGrid`. So 3 of the 4 destructured names resolve to `undefined`.
findings.whyConcern: `hexToPixel(...)`, `pixelToHex(...)`, and `getHexCorners(...)` called off the composable instance throw `TypeError: not a function`. The correct instance methods are `getHexPosition` (axial→pixel) and `getHexPoints` (SVG polygon).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Hex board authors (Hex is the flagship 'simplest' example game) copy the snippet and crash; the names also collide confusingly with the real standalone exports of the same name, masking the mistake.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ui-components.md:985-991 — align destructured names with useHexGrid.ts:85-119 (`getHexPosition`, `getHexPoints`, `getNeighbors`, `hexDistance`), or show the standalone `hexToPixel`/`getHexPolygonPoints` import path instead of pulling them off the composable.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. docs/ui-components.md:985-989 destructures `{ hexToPixel, pixelToHex, getHexCorners, getNeighbors }` from `useHexGrid({size:30, orientation:'flat'})`. The composable useHexGrid (defined at src/ui/composables/useHexGrid.ts:138) returns the HexGridReturn interface (src/ui/composables/useHexGrid.ts:84-121), which exposes getHexPosition (:104), getHexPoints (:106), getNeighbors (:116), hexDistance (:114), axialToCube/cubeToAxial (:110-112), etc. It contains NO hexToPixel, NO pixelToHex, and NO getHexCorners member. grep confirms hexToPixel exists only as a standalone export at :375, while pixelToHex and getHexCorners do not appear anywhere in the file. Therefore 3 of the 4 destructured names resolve to undefined and would throw TypeError if called off the composable instance. The finding's citations are accurate and the problem is real. I downgrade severity from medium to low because the defect is confined to one documentation snippet (no shipped/runtime code is broken) and the snippet is internally inconsistent — its very next line at :996 calls hexToPixel(3,2,30,'flat') using the standalone 4-arg signature, signaling the destructure block is simply an incorrect doc fragment rather than a load-bearing API contract.

findings.title: dice-and-scoring.md shows UniqueTrack pointsPerEntry as a function, but the API only accepts number[]
findings.evidence: docs/dice-and-scoring.md:382-387 constructs `new UniqueTrack({ id: 'ones', name: 'Ones', maxEntries: 1, pointsPerEntry: (value) => value, // Function-based points })`. The config type forbids this: src/engine/scoring/track.ts:55 `pointsPerEntry?: number[]`, stored at :87 `this.pointsPerEntry = config.pointsPerEntry ?? new Array(...).fill(0)` and indexed as an array at :121/:137 (`this.pointsPerEntry[position]`). `UniqueTrackConfig` (track.ts:304-307) extends TrackConfig and adds only `validRange` — it does NOT widen `pointsPerEntry` to a function.
findings.whyConcern: Passing a function is a TypeScript error, and at runtime `this.pointsPerEntry[position]` on a function is `undefined`, so `add()` returns the `?? 0` fallback — every entry silently scores 0. The 'Function-based points' comment is doubly misleading because no such feature exists.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A roll-and-write designer building a Yahtzee-style upper section follows the doc, gets a type error or (in JS) a track that always scores zero, with no hint that only a numeric array is supported.
findings.fixPaths: docs/dice-and-scoring.md:382-387 — change to `pointsPerEntry: [1]` (or the appropriate numeric array) and drop the 'Function-based points' claim; or, if function-based points are desired, implement and type it in src/engine/scoring/track.ts before documenting it.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified against current source. docs/dice-and-scoring.md:386 constructs a UniqueTrack with `pointsPerEntry: (value) => value, // Function-based points`. The accepted type is `number[]`: TrackConfig.pointsPerEntry is declared `number[]` at src/engine/scoring/track.ts:55, assigned at :87 (`config.pointsPerEntry ?? new Array(config.maxEntries).fill(0)`), and read as an array at :121 and :137 (`this.pointsPerEntry[position]`). UniqueTrackConfig at track.ts:304-307 extends TrackConfig and adds only `validRange`; it does NOT widen pointsPerEntry to a function. Therefore the documented snippet is a TypeScript error, and in plain JS the function gets stored and `this.pointsPerEntry[position]` is `undefined`, so `?? 0` makes every entry silently score 0. The 'Function-based points' comment advertises a capability that does not exist. Real docs-mismatch. I rate it medium rather than higher because TypeScript users get a compile error (not a silent runtime failure), but it is an authoritative API example targeting the exact Yahtzee upper-section use case, so it actively misleads designers.

findings.title: client.md documents a fabricated GameConnection API — ~12 methods that do not exist in source
findings.evidence: docs/api/client.md examples call connection.onConnect() (line 141), onDisconnect() (145), onActionResult() (157), onLobbyUpdate() (243), claimPosition() (224), updateName() (231), setReady() (237), undo() (278), restart() (285), getHistory() (195), getStateAt() (199), getStateDiff() (202), and client.matchmakingStatus() (117). Grep of src/client/game-connection.ts returns 0 for onConnect, onDisconnect, onActionResult, onLobbyUpdate, claimPosition, updateName, setReady, restart, getHistory, getStateAt, getStateDiff, and 0 for `undo`. The real GameConnection methods are onConnectionChange (game-connection.ts:198), onLobbyChange (game-connection.ts:178), action (117), getState (105), getStatus (109). The lobby/history/restart operations live on MeepleClient instead: claimSeat (client.ts:336), updateLobbyName (367), setReady(gameId, ready) (386), restartGame (298), getHistory(gameId) (277), and getMatchStatus (82, not 'matchmakingStatus').
findings.whyConcern: Nearly every connection example in the client SDK reference is uncallable. Code copied from these examples throws 'is not a function' at runtime. The doc also conflates client-level operations (lobby/restart/history) with connection-level ones, so even a careful reader cannot infer the correct object.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: This is the canonical browser-SDK reference. A game developer or AI agent following it writes connection.claimPosition(...)/onLobbyUpdate(...)/undo(...) and gets runtime failures; the easy path (copy the doc) is the broken path.
findings.fixPaths: docs/api/client.md: rewrite the Connection/Lobby/History/Undo/Restart examples to use the real surface — connection.onConnectionChange/onLobbyChange/action/getState, and MeepleClient.claimSeat/updateLobbyName/setReady/restartGame/getHistory/getMatchStatus. Cross-check every method name against src/client/game-connection.ts and src/client/client.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed accurate. docs/api/client.md calls connection.onConnect (141), onDisconnect (145), onActionResult (157), onLobbyUpdate (243), claimPosition (224), updateName (231), setReady (237), getHistory (195), getStateAt (199), getStateDiff (202), undo (278), restart (285) — verified by reading the file. Grep of src/client/game-connection.ts returns 0 for all of these; the class only defines getState (105), getStatus (109), action (117), onStateChange (167), onLobbyChange (178), onError (193), onConnectionChange (198). The lobby/history/restart ops actually live on MeepleClient in src/client/client.ts: getMatchStatus (82, doc wrongly calls it client.matchmakingStatus and passes an arg it doesn't take), getHistory(gameId) (277), restartGame (298), claimSeat (336), updateLobbyName (367), setReady(gameId,ready) (386). So the doc invents ~12-13 connection methods that do not exist and conflates client-level operations with connection-level ones. Copying the examples throws 'is not a function' at runtime. This is the canonical browser-SDK reference, so impact is broad; high is appropriate (docs-only, no library code defect, so not critical).

findings.title: runtime.md GameRunner examples use executeAction/getActionHistory/getStateAt — none exist; real method is performAction with positional args
findings.evidence: docs/api/runtime.md line 78 and lines 217-219 call runner.executeAction({ action, player, args }); line 222 calls runner.getActionHistory(); line 226 calls runner.getStateAt(2). Grep of src/runtime/runner.ts for executeAction|getActionHistory|getStateAt returns 0. The actual signature is performAction(actionName: string, player: Player|number, args: Record<string,unknown>): ActionExecutionResult (src/runtime/runner.ts:121-124) — positional arguments, not an object literal. Public methods are start, captureCheckpoint, performAction, getFlowState, getSnapshot, getPlayerView, getAllPlayerViews, isComplete, getWinners (runner.ts:93-258).
findings.whyConcern: The headline 'Using GameRunner' example is wrong on both the method name and the calling convention. runner.executeAction({action:'move',player:0,args:{}}) fails, and the correct call is runner.performAction('move', 0, {}). getActionHistory/getStateAt simply don't exist.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: runtime is the low-level execution API used by client/server integrators. The reference teaches an object-shaped call that the positional API rejects, sending integrators down a debugging dead-end.
findings.fixPaths: docs/api/runtime.md: replace executeAction({action,player,args}) with performAction(actionName, player, args) everywhere; remove or replace getActionHistory()/getStateAt() with real methods (getSnapshot/getFlowState) per src/runtime/runner.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed against current source. docs/api/runtime.md:78 and 217-219 call runner.executeAction({ action, player, args }) with an object literal; line 222 calls runner.getActionHistory(); line 226 calls runner.getStateAt(2). Grep of src/runtime/runner.ts for executeAction|getActionHistory|getStateAt returns 0 hits — the only match is performAction (line 121) and an internal usage (line 274). The real signature at runner.ts:121-125 is performAction(actionName: string, player: Player | number, args: Record<string, unknown>): ActionExecutionResult — positional arguments, not an object literal, and the method is named performAction not executeAction. getActionHistory and getStateAt do not exist on GameRunner. Both 'Using GameRunner' and 'Runner with Action History' examples are fully broken: wrong method name, wrong calling convention, and two phantom methods. Finding is real and accurately cited. I lower severity from high to medium: this is documentation-only (no shipped code bug), and the doc itself notes runtime is consumed internally by client/server, so the direct-integrator audience is narrow; still a genuine copy-paste-fails reference error.

findings.title: ai-system.md GameSession example uses a private constructor, wrong option names, and a nonexistent start()
findings.evidence: docs/ai-system.md:184 does `new GameSession({ gameClass: MyGame, ..., aiPlayers: [1], aiLevel: 'hard', aiConfig: ... })` then calls `session.start()` (line ~196). But src/session/game-session.ts:171 declares `private constructor(...)` — `new GameSession(...)` is a compile error; the only entry point is the static GameSession.create (game-session.ts:248). GameSessionOptions (game-session.ts:59-79) has fields GameClass (capital G), playerNames, and aiConfig: AIConfig — there are no gameClass/aiPlayers/aiLevel fields. Grep for a public `start(` in game-session.ts returns nothing (the runner has start(), not the session).
findings.whyConcern: The entire 'Integration with GameSession' snippet cannot compile or run: private constructor, three invented option keys, and a missing start() method. The correct form (per docs/api/session.md:93) is GameSession.create({ GameClass, gameType, playerCount, playerNames, aiConfig: { players: [1], level: 'hard' } }).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: ai-system.md is the primary guide for wiring AI into a session. An AI agent or designer copying it produces non-compiling code and may conclude the API is broken, when they simply followed a fabricated example.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ai-system.md: change `new GameSession({...})` to `GameSession.create({...})`, rename gameClass->GameClass, replace aiPlayers/aiLevel with aiConfig: { players, level }, add playerNames, and drop session.start(). Mirror docs/api/session.md and src/session/game-session.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Every claim checks out against current source.

1. docs/ai-system.md:184-194 (the "Integration with GameSession" snippet) does `new GameSession({ gameClass: MyGame, gameType: 'my-game', playerCount: 2, aiPlayers: [1], aiLevel: 'hard', aiConfig: myGameAIConfig })` and then `session.start();` at line 194. Confirmed verbatim.

2. src/session/game-session.ts:171 declares `private constructor(` — so `new GameSession(...)` is a compile error. The only public entry point is `static create<G>` at line 248. Confirmed.

3. GameSessionOptions (game-session.ts:59-83) has `GameClass` (capital G, line 61), `playerNames` (line 63), and `aiConfig?: AIConfig` (line 67). There are NO `gameClass`, `aiPlayers`, or `aiLevel` top-level fields. Three of the four keys in the example are fabricated, and `gameClass` is mis-cased. Confirmed.

4. No public `start(` method exists in game-session.ts — `grep -nE "^\s+(public|async)?start\("` returns nothing (exit 1). The static `create` returns a session that begins the game; there is no `.start()`. Confirmed.

5. The correct form is documented in docs/api/session.md:93: `GameSession.create({ gameType, GameClass: MyGame, playerCount, playerNames: [...] })`. Confirmed.

The entire canonical AI-integration snippet cannot compile: private constructor + three invented option keys + nonexistent start(). Since ai-system.md is the primary guide for wiring AI into a session and a copy-paste of this example fails on four distinct counts, high severity is warranted (docs-only, but the flagship example for the feature is fully broken).
