The actual exports are `ClaimSeatRequest`/`ClaimSeatResponse`, confirmed at src/client/index.ts:78-79 and src/session/index.ts:69-70. So `import type { ClaimPositionRequest } from 'boardsmith/client'` (or '/session') would fail to resolve, which is a genuine docs-vs-code mismatch. I downgrade severity from medium to low: this is documentation-only naming drift affecting two type-list entries; impact is limited to an integrator getting an unresolved-import that is quickly self-diagnosed (the correct 'Seat' term is one grep away), with no runtime or correctness consequence in the library itself.

findings.title: ai-system.md imports `Game` from 'boardsmith/ai', which does not export it
findings.evidence: docs/ai-system.md:84 and :137 use `import type { AIConfig, Game } from 'boardsmith/ai';`. src/ai/index.ts only exports MCTSBot, createBot, parseAILevel, DIFFICULTY_PRESETS, DEFAULT_CONFIG and types BotConfig/BotMove/AIConfig/Objective/ThreatResponse/DifficultyLevel (src/ai/index.ts:7-16, 45, 72). `Game` is only used as an internal generic bound (imported from '../engine' at index.ts:1) and is never re-exported — grep `export .*\bGame\b` in src/ai/index.ts matches only `export function createBot<G extends Game>`.
findings.whyConcern: The import line fails to resolve `Game` from 'boardsmith/ai'. Game is exported from the root 'boardsmith' package, not from the ai subpath.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: AI-feature authors copying the objective/feature typing example hit an unresolved import; the right path (import Game from 'boardsmith') is not discoverable from this doc.
findings.fixPaths: docs/ai-system.md:84,137: import Game from 'boardsmith' (or add a re-export of Game in src/ai/index.ts if a single-import surface is desired).
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified accurate. docs/ai-system.md:84 and :137 both use `import type { AIConfig, Game } from 'boardsmith/ai';`. package.json exports maps "./ai" to src/ai/index.ts. That file (Read in full) re-exports only MCTSBot (7), types BotConfig/BotMove/AIConfig/Objective/ThreatResponse/DifficultyLevel (8-15), DIFFICULTY_PRESETS/DEFAULT_CONFIG (16), and functions createBot (45)/parseAILevel (72). `Game` is imported from '../engine/index.js' at line 1 and used only as an internal generic bound (lines 19, 45); it is never re-exported, so `import { Game } from 'boardsmith/ai'` fails to resolve. The correct source is the root 'boardsmith' entry: src/engine/index.ts exports Game (line 18), and package.json maps '.' to src/engine/index.ts. The docs example is therefore broken — copying it yields a TS unresolved-import error, and the discoverable right path (import Game from 'boardsmith') is not shown. Real docs-mismatch. I rate it low rather than the finder's medium: it is documentation-only, affects a single symbol on an otherwise-valid import line, and surfaces as an immediate, clear compile error rather than silent runtime breakage.

dimensions.summary: The Pit-of-Success dimension shows systemic erosion: the codebase repeatedly makes the wrong path the easy path and the right path the deliberate one. Security and correctness default the wrong way — Deck and Hand expose their contents unless the designer remembers an extra opt-in call, chooseElements silently drops invalid IDs and never enforces multiSelect min/max, and the Client SDK mints player-identity tokens with Math.random(); meanwhile the state layer harbors high-severity recording gaps where completed pending/repeating actions never reach actionHistory and undo computes turn boundaries with a heuristic the code itself flags as broken, so replay, undo counts, and the canUndo display silently disagree. A strong "fail silent" anti-pattern runs throughout: loop()'s maxIterations completes instead of erroring, onSelect and simulateRandomGames swallow errors while still committing or reporting success, random simulation emits a seed that can't reproduce its own failure, and documented-but-inert features ('unordered' visibility, triggerChoiceSelect, registerBeforeAutoExecute, useDragDrop outside a provider) present capabilities that quietly do nothing — each a trap that hides bugs rather than surfacing them. APIs also overstate their generality (useDragDrop auto-wires only two hard-coded shapes; WebSocketMessage is a stringly-typed all-optional bag) and lose structured information already in hand, as when GameSession re-derives errorCode by substring-matching such that NOT_YOUR_TURN is never produced. Not everything is broken — useBoardInteraction correctly throws outside its provider (a good fail-loud model its drag-drop sibling should follow), the runtime honestly documents action-replay restore as unsound, and structured error codes exist at the runner layer — but these healthy patterns are inconsistently applied, leaving the overall surface tilted toward quiet failure and insecure defaults that demand vigilance rather than rewarding it.

findings.title: chooseElements multiSelect min/max constraints are never enforced server-side; invalid element IDs are silently dropped
findings.evidence: action/action.ts:660-711 (validateSelection 'elements' branch) only checks that each submitted element is a *valid choice* and not disabled — it never checks array length against the selection's declared count bounds. The `multiSelect` field is read nowhere in validation: grep shows it appears in action.ts only inside a comment at action/action.ts:624. The declared config (action/types.ts:107-111 `MultiSelectConfig { min?, max? }`, documented e.g. `multiSelect: { min: 3, max: 3 }` at action/types.ts:191) is therefore advisory to the UI only. Worse, in resolveArgs (action/action.ts:188-198) a multi-select array maps unknown IDs through `getElementById(...)` and `.filter((el): el is GameElement => el !== null)`, so any ID that doesn't resolve is silently removed before validation/execute.
findings.whyConcern: A designer writes `chooseElements('cards', { multiSelect: { min: 2, max: 2 } })` and trusts the engine to guarantee exactly two cards in `execute`. A bot, test harness, or custom UI that submits `{ cards: [] }`, `{ cards: [a,b,c,d] }`, or `{ cards: [validId, 9999] }` passes validation; execute() then runs with an out-of-bounds (or silently shortened) array, corrupting game state with no error.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The obvious, declarative way to bound a selection (the `multiSelect` option, prominently documented with min/max examples) does nothing on the authoritative server path. The easy path looks correct but silently isn't, and the failure only surfaces as downstream state corruption far from the cause.
findings.fixPaths: In action/action.ts validateSelection, resolve the selection's multiSelect config (handling number | {min,max} | function) and push errors when the array length is below min or above max, mirroring the text/number bound checks at action.ts:721-748. In resolveArgs (action.ts:188-198), do not silently drop unresolved IDs — collect them and let validateSelection reject the submission with an actionable 'Element ID N not found' error.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real on current source. validateSelection's 'elements' branch (action.ts:660-711) only checks each submitted element is a valid, non-disabled choice; it never checks array length against declared multiSelect min/max. grep confirms `multiSelect` appears in action.ts solely as a comment at line 624 — validation never reads it. resolveArgs (action.ts:186-198) maps multi-select IDs and applies `.filter((el): el is GameElement => el !== null)`, silently dropping unresolvable IDs before validation, so `[validId, 9999]` resolves to `[validElement]` and passes. MultiSelectConfig {min?,max?} exists and is prominently documented (types.ts:107-111, 191). The session layer (pick-handler.ts:247-266, 298-311) evaluates the config only to forward {min,max} to the client for rendering; no submission-side enforcement. executeAction (819-863) goes resolveArgs → validateAction → execute with the count never enforced. By contrast number (740-744) and text (721-728) selections DO enforce min/max, making the gap a clear inconsistency. The default chooseElements is multiSelect:{min:1} (action-builder.ts:459) yet an empty array [] still passes (neither undefined nor null, and the validate for-loop has no items to reject), so even the default is unenforced. Tests (action.test.ts:2024-2030) only assert the config is stored, never that it's enforced. Severity high: the official Action Panel guards via the forwarded config, but AI-trainer/MCTS bots, test harnesses, and custom UIs are first-class parity paths per CLAUDE.md and bypass that guard, reaching execute() with out-of-bounds/silently-shortened arrays and corrupting game state far from the cause.

findings.title: Deck and Hand default to fully-visible contents (opt-in security), so hidden information leaks unless the designer remembers an extra call
findings.evidence: Deck (element/deck.ts:44-56) and Hand (element/hand.ts:40-51) constructors set only layout properties ($direction/$fan/$overlap) and never set zone visibility. The default visibility is `DEFAULT_VISIBILITY = { mode: 'all' }` (command/visibility.ts:62-65), and resolveVisibility falls back to that 'all' default for any element/zone without explicit visibility (command/visibility.ts:97-99). So a Deck/Hand's children serialize fully (faces and order) into every player's `toJSONForPlayer` (element/game.ts:2231 onward only hides zones whose mode is explicitly hidden/owner/count-only). The docstrings themselves show the required follow-up calls `deck.contentsHidden()` (deck.ts:24) and `hand.contentsVisibleToOwner()` (hand.ts:21) as separate steps.
findings.whyConcern: `Deck` and `Hand` are the library's canonical containers for hidden information (draw piles, player hands). Creating one the obvious way — `game.create(Deck, 'draw')` then populate/shuffle — yields a deck whose card identities AND shuffle order are visible to all opponents. Single-player/dev testing looks fine; the leak only manifests in real multiplayer and is invisible to the designer because nothing errors.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Directly violates the project's stated rule 'Secure defaults, not opt-in security.' The element name promises secrecy but the default behavior is full disclosure; correctness requires remembering a separate, easy-to-forget call. Wrong (leaky) usage is the path of least resistance.
findings.fixPaths: Default Deck contents to hidden and Hand contents to owner-only in their constructors (element/deck.ts, element/hand.ts) via setZoneVisibility, letting designers opt into more visibility explicitly. This makes the secure configuration the zero-effort default while keeping override methods (contentsVisible(), etc.) available.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified on current source (paths are under src/engine/, which the finding omitted, but files are correctly identified). Deck constructor (src/engine/element/deck.ts:44-56) sets only order and layout ($direction/$overlap/$align); Hand constructor (src/engine/element/hand.ts:40-51) sets only fan/layout. Neither calls setZoneVisibility, so getZoneVisibility() returns the unset this._zoneVisibility (space.ts:140). DEFAULT_VISIBILITY = {mode:'all'} (command/visibility.ts:62-65) and resolveVisibility falls back to it (visibility.ts:97-99). In toJSONForPlayer (element/game.ts:2231-2307), children are only anonymized when zoneVisibility mode is hidden/count-only/owner (lines 2235-2288); with no zone visibility set, control reaches the 'Filter children normally' branch (2291-2307) that serializes full child names and order to every player. The helper methods contentsHidden()/contentsVisibleToOwner() exist (space.ts:153-162) and are shown in the docstrings (deck.ts:23, hand.ts:20) as separate, easy-to-forget steps. This is a genuine pit-of-success violation: Deck (draw pile) and Hand are the canonical hidden-information containers, yet creating them the obvious way leaks card identities and shuffle order to opponents, with no error — a silent multiplayer-only leak that directly contradicts the project's stated 'Secure defaults, not opt-in security' rule. Severity high: the named subclasses semantically promise secrecy but default to full disclosure, and the failure is invisible during single-player/dev testing. (Slightly below critical only because the library does provide and document the mitigating calls and base-Space 'all' default is defensible for visible zones like discard piles.)

findings.title: loop()'s maxIterations safety limit silently completes the loop instead of failing loud
findings.evidence: executeLoop (flow/engine.ts:1031-1038): when `iteration >= maxIterations` it sets `frame.completed = true` and returns `{ continue: true }` with no warning or error — identical handling to the normal `while`-became-false termination at engine.ts:1040-1043. By contrast the global runaway guard in run() (engine.ts:907-927) throws a detailed error. Designers are actively nudged to add maxIterations: builders.ts:95-104 warns 'loop(...) has no maxIterations set' and tells them to 'Add maxIterations to set a safety limit: loop({ maxIterations: 100, ... })'.
findings.whyConcern: A designer follows the documented advice and adds `maxIterations: 100` to silence the warning. If their `while` predicate has a bug and never becomes false, the loop stops silently at 100 and the flow proceeds as though the loop finished normally — producing an invalid game state (e.g. a round that should have continued just ends) with zero diagnostics.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The recommended 'safety limit' converts a loud, debuggable infinite-loop into a silent wrong-state. The guidance leads designers toward the value that hides the bug; the loud global guard (10000 iters) is the only thing that ever surfaces a problem, and an explicit small maxIterations suppresses even that.
findings.fixPaths: In executeLoop (flow/engine.ts:1035), when the limit is hit, throw (or at minimum devWarn loudly) with the loop name and iteration count — only treat `while` returning false as clean completion. Distinguish 'hit safety cap' from 'condition satisfied' so the cap behaves like an assertion, not a normal exit.
findings.verifierReasoning: All citations verified on current source. executeLoop (engine.ts:1035-1038) silently sets frame.completed=true and returns {continue:true} when iteration>=maxIterations — identical handling to the natural while-false termination at 1040-1043, with no warning or error. The global guard in run() (engine.ts:907-927) DOES throw a detailed infinite-loop error at iterations>DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS (=10000, engine.ts:30). builders.ts:95-106 emits a devWarn telling designers to add maxIterations:100 as a 'safety limit'. The pit-of-success concern is real: because each loop iteration consumes several run() iterations, the loud global guard normally fires first when no explicit maxIterations is set; but once a designer follows the documented advice and sets a small explicit maxIterations, executeLoop terminates silently before the global guard can trip. A buggy while predicate that never goes false then stops silently at the limit and the flow proceeds with invalid state and zero diagnostics — the recommended remedy converts a loud failure into a silent one. I lower severity from high to medium: it only manifests when the while condition is already buggy, and loop legitimately supports maxIterations-as-count (so unconditionally throwing isn't trivially correct), making this a debuggability/observability defect rather than a correctness regression of well-written code. A distinct log/warning when the explicit maxIterations safety limit is actually reached would resolve it.

findings.title: 'unordered' visibility mode is documented and typed but silently behaves identically to 'hidden'
findings.evidence: VisibilityMode includes 'unordered' documented as 'Players can see contents but not order (e.g., shuffled deck peek)' (command/visibility.ts:8-10). But canPlayerSee returns false for 'unordered' (command/visibility.ts:51-53, grouped with 'hidden'/'count-only'), and toJSONForPlayer special-cases only 'hidden', 'count-only', and 'owner' zone modes (element/game.ts:2236, 2265) — there is no 'unordered' branch, so children inherit the 'unordered' zone, hit `!element.isVisibleTo(...)` and are replaced with `{ __hidden: true }` placeholders (game.ts:2222-2228). grep confirms 'unordered' appears in no logic outside visibility.ts.
findings.whyConcern: A designer who calls `setZoneVisibility('unordered')` (or a piece's equivalent) expecting players to see card identities in a randomized order instead gets fully-hidden placeholders — the exact opposite of the documented contract — with no error or warning.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An invalid state is representable and reachable through a typed, documented API that doesn't do what it says. The designer is lured into a feature that silently no-ops into 'hidden', and discovers it only by inspecting client payloads.
findings.fixPaths: Either implement 'unordered' in toJSONForPlayer (element/game.ts) — emit real child contents but in shuffled/order-stripped form — and adjust canPlayerSee accordingly, or remove 'unordered' from VisibilityMode (command/visibility.ts:10) until it's supported so it can't be selected.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source (paths are under src/engine/, finder omitted the engine/ prefix but line numbers match). visibility.ts:8-10 documents 'unordered' as "Players can see contents but not order" and exports it in VisibilityMode. canPlayerSee (visibility.ts:50-53) groups 'unordered' with 'hidden'/'count-only' returning false. game.ts toJSONForPlayer: the zone-children special-cases handle only 'hidden'/'count-only' (:2236) and 'owner' (:2265); 'unordered' falls through to "filter children normally" (:2291). Children inherit the zone's visibility via game-element.ts:617-623 getEffectiveVisibility → resolveVisibility(undefined, {mode:'unordered'}), so each child resolves to mode 'unordered', fails isVisibleTo (:2222), and is replaced with a bare {__hidden:true} placeholder (:2224-2228). Reachable via public setZoneVisibility(mode: VisibilityMode) (space.ts:132) and piece.setVisibility (piece.ts:150). grep confirms 'unordered' exists in no logic outside visibility.ts. The documented, typed API silently behaves identically to 'hidden' (actually slightly worse — the bare placeholder doesn't even preserve childCount/$-attrs like the real hidden-zone branch). This is a genuine pit-of-success contract violation. I downgrade finder's 'medium' to 'low': the failure mode over-hides (fails safe, no information leak or crash), 'unordered' is a niche advanced mode with no convenience method nudging designers toward it, so likelihood and blast radius are limited.

findings.title: onSelect callback errors are swallowed and the action still commits
findings.evidence: In executeAction (action/action.ts:838-846) each selection's onSelect is invoked inside try/catch that only `console.error`s and then falls through to `action.execute(...)` at action.ts:854-855. The same swallow-and-continue pattern is in the step path (action.ts:1531-1545). onCancel (the documented compensating hook) only fires on explicit cancellation (fireOnCancelCallbacks, action.ts:1556-1570), never on a thrown onSelect.
findings.whyConcern: onSelect is documented for side effects ('Called after this step is resolved'), with onCancel provided to undo them. If onSelect throws after partially mutating state, the error is logged but the action proceeds to execute() on top of that half-applied mutation, and onCancel never runs — leaving inconsistent state with no failure surfaced to the player or caller.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The natural use of onSelect (mutate/reserve state when a step resolves) becomes unsafe: a bug inside it doesn't abort the action, it silently corrupts state. Fail-fast is the documented project norm ('Fail fast and loud, not silently'); here a misuse is quietly absorbed.
findings.fixPaths: In action/action.ts, when onSelect throws, abort the action: fire any already-registered onCancel callbacks and return `{ success: false, error }` instead of logging and continuing (action.ts:840-846 and 1532-1545).
findings.verifierReasoning:
Citations verified against current source at /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/action/action.ts (finder's cited path src/action/action.ts is wrong, but actual path is src/engine/action/action.ts — content matches exactly).

(1) executeAction path: lines 838-846 invoke each selection's onSelect inside try/catch whose only handler is `console.error(...)` (843). Execution then unconditionally falls through to `action.execute(resolvedArgs, context)` at 854-855. Confirmed: a thrown onSelect does NOT abort; execute() runs on top of whatever partial mutation occurred.

(2) Step path: lines 1531-1545 (validateAndStoreSelection) — same swallow pattern: onSelect in try/catch, catch only console.errors (1543), then currentSelectionIndex++ and returns success (1547-1549). A third identical instance exists in the repeat path at 1318-1335 that the finder didn't cite, reinforcing the pattern.

(3) onCancel is the documented compensating hook (action-builder.ts:219 'Called if the action is cancelled after onSelect fired but before execute()'). fireOnCancelCallbacks (1556-1570) only iterates pendingState.onSelectFired and is invoked only on explicit cancellation — it never runs in response to a thrown onSelect. Confirmed.

The documented contract (action-builder.ts:217 'Called after this step is resolved'; onCancel exists specifically to undo onSelect side effects) establishes that onSelect is expected to carry side effects. OnSelectContext (action.ts:99-106) only exposes animate(), but game-state mutation via closure capture of the game object is still possible — and onCancel's existence proves side effects are an intended use. So a throwing onSelect leaves half-applied state, proceeds to execute() on top of it, and never runs onCancel compensation, with the only signal being a console.error. This directly violates the project's documented 'Fail fast and loud, not silently' norm and is a genuine pit-of-success problem.

Severity medium rather than high: triggering requires a bug inside user-supplied onSelect, and the restricted context discourages (but doesn't prevent) mutation. But when triggered, the failure mode is silent state corruption with no error surfaced to player or caller — exactly the hard-to-debug class the project norm targets. Concur with finder's medium.

findings.title: Drag-and-drop only auto-wires for two hard-coded action shapes, but useDragDrop presents itself as a general API
findings.evidence: All drop-target wiring lives exclusively in ActionPanel's `watch(() => boardInteraction?.isDragging)` (src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue:753). It recognizes only Case 2 — element-type first selection + choice second selection WITH filterBy (ActionPanel.vue:809-827) — and Case 3 — element + element (ActionPanel.vue:886-890). Any other valid shape (element->choice WITHOUT filterBy, 'elements' multi types, choice->element, single chooseElement onto a zone) falls through to a devWarn and the drag silently does nothing (ActionPanel.vue:892-906). Meanwhile `useDragDrop`'s docstring and `drag({id})`/`drop({name})` API (src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:7-29, 160-168) present drag as fully general, and `setDropTargets` is never called anywhere except those three ActionPanel branches (grep: only ActionPanel.vue:795,870,934).
findings.whyConcern: A custom UI built with useDragDrop works for chess-like (element->filterBy-choice) and card-to-zone (element->element) actions but silently fails for every other action shape, even though the ActionPanel renders working buttons for the exact same action. This directly violates the CLAUDE.md hard rule 'All UI interactions must work in a Custom UI and Action Panel in parity through useBoardInteraction.'
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A game designer wires `useDragDrop` against the documented general API, sees it work for one action, then a structurally different action's drag does nothing with only a console devWarn. The easy path (use the published drag API) is not the right path unless the action happens to match one of two undocumented internal patterns.
findings.fixPaths: Move drop-target derivation out of ActionPanel into the shared controller/board-interaction layer so any action's current pick exposes drop targets (e.g., derive dropTargets from the current element/choice pick's validElements/choices regardless of ordering or filterBy), and have useDragDrop/AutoElement consume that. Alternatively, document the two supported patterns explicitly and make the devWarn enumerate the unsupported shape. Files: src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue, src/ui/composables/useBoardInteraction.ts, src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: All factual citations verified against current source. ActionPanel.vue:753 holds the sole drag orchestrator (`watch(() => boardInteraction?.isDragging)`). Grep confirms `setDropTargets` is called nowhere in consumer code except ActionPanel.vue:795, 870, 934 (the rest are the interface declaration/implementation in useBoardInteraction.ts:136/307 and one test). The branch conditions confirm only two action shapes auto-wire: element→choice WITH filterBy (Case 1 line 767-770, Case 2 line 826 requires `secondSel.filterBy`) and element→element (Case 3 line 889). I traced each shape the finder lists as unsupported and confirmed they all fall through to the `devWarn` at ActionPanel.vue:894-906: element→choice without filterBy (Case 2 line 826 rejects), 'elements' multi-type first-select with no action in progress (Case 2 line 814 and Case 3 line 889 both require `=== 'element'`), choice→element (both require firstSel element), and single-selection-onto-zone (both require a secondSel). The useDragDrop.ts docstring (lines 4-5, 7-29, 160-168) does advertise the API as working with 'any element' with automatic orchestration and does NOT disclose the two-pattern restriction, so the parity gap with ActionPanel buttons (CLAUDE.md hard rule) is real for unsupported shapes. The finding is genuine and the citations are accurate. I lowered severity from high to medium because two of the finder's framing claims are overstated: (1) the limitation is explicitly documented — docs/common-pitfalls.md section 19 is dedicated to it and docs/ui-components.md:507/660 list the two patterns; (2) the failure is not silent — the devWarn at ActionPanel.vue:894 emits an actionable message naming both supported patterns plus current/available actions. The two supported patterns also cover the most common board-game cases (move piece to square, drag card to zone). The un-mitigated core remains: the composable's own IDE-facing docstring claims generality with no type-level enforcement, a legitimate pit-of-success concern, but the documentation and dev-warning mitigations make medium the appropriate severity.

findings.title: useDragDrop silently no-ops outside a GameShell provider while its sibling useBoardInteraction throws
findings.evidence: `useDragDrop` resolves the provider via `tryUseBoardInteraction()` (src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:176) and every handler early-returns when it is absent: dragProps `if (!boardInteraction) return` (line 181), dropProps `if (!boardInteraction?.isDragging) return` (lines 229-253), and isDragging/isDropTarget/isOver return false (lines 259-272). By contrast `useBoardInteraction()` throws a descriptive error when no provider exists (src/ui/composables/useBoardInteraction.ts:404-414).
findings.whyConcern: A custom board that renders outside the GameShell provider (or in a test harness) gets completely non-functional drag-and-drop with zero diagnostics — no thrown error, no warning. The two sibling injection APIs fail in opposite ways.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The wrong path (using drag without the provider) is silent, so a developer sees 'nothing happens when I drag' and has no signal pointing at the missing GameShell context — the opposite of fail-fast-and-loud.
findings.fixPaths: Make useDragDrop call useBoardInteraction() (throwing) by default, or emit a one-time devWarn the first time a drag handler runs with no provider. File: src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:175-272.
findings.verifierReasoning: Citations confirmed on current source. useDragDrop (src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:176) uses tryUseBoardInteraction() and silently early-returns in every handler when the provider is absent: dragstart line 181, dropProps guards lines 230/237/253, isDragging/isDropTarget/isOver return false at lines 260/265/270. By contrast useBoardInteraction() throws a descriptive error (src/ui/composables/useBoardInteraction.ts:404-414), whose own docstring states it mirrors injectActionController() so siblings "fail the same way instead of one silently returning undefined." The asymmetry is real and acknowledged in code. The pit-of-success concern is legitimate: a custom board outside GameShell gets non-functional drag with no error/warning, the opposite of fail-fast — and useDragDrop is documented for custom UIs that are always inside GameShell, so the graceful-degradation rationale for tryUse does not clearly apply here. I downgrade from medium to low because this only manifests under misconfiguration or test harnesses (normal custom UIs always render inside GameShell), and there is no functional/data corruption in production paths — it is purely a diagnostics/DX gap.

findings.title: triggerChoiceSelect (the documented custom-UI choice path) silently no-ops unless an action is already active AND the pick name matches
findings.evidence: `triggerChoiceSelect` only fires if `state.onChoiceSelect` is set (src/ui/composables/useBoardInteraction.ts:370-374). That callback is installed solely by ActionPanel's `watch(currentAction)` and only when an action is truthy; when null it sets the callback to null (ActionPanel.vue:649-668). The installed callback further guards `if (currentPick.value?.name === selectionName)` before doing anything (ActionPanel.vue:660-663). In contrast, clicking an element on the board CAN auto-start an action via the selectedElement watch (ActionPanel.vue:686-750).
findings.whyConcern: A custom UI that calls `boardInteraction.triggerChoiceSelect('suit','H')` to begin or out-of-order-fill a choice gets a silent no-op, while the element-click equivalent auto-starts the action. The 'shared state' contract implies either side can drive selection, but choices only work when ActionPanel has pre-armed the callback for exactly the current pick.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The documented method (useBoardInteraction.ts:170 'called by custom UI for non-element choices like suit selection') appears to be the right path, but it fails silently in common timing scenarios, luring designers into thinking their wiring is wrong rather than that the API only works mid-action.
findings.fixPaths: Either auto-start the matching action when triggerChoiceSelect is called with no active action (mirroring the element auto-start), or have triggerChoiceSelect surface a devWarn when onChoiceSelect is null / pick name mismatches. Files: src/ui/composables/useBoardInteraction.ts:370-374, src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue:649-668.
findings.verifierReasoning: All file:line citations verified accurate against current source. useBoardInteraction.ts:370-374: `triggerChoiceSelect` only invokes `state.onChoiceSelect` if it is set, otherwise silently returns. The callback is installed exclusively by ActionPanel.vue:649-668 inside `watch(currentAction)` — set only when `action` is truthy, and set to `null` when the action is null (lines 664-667). The installed callback adds a further guard `if (currentPick.value?.name === selectionName)` (lines 660-663) before calling `setSelectionValue`. By contrast, the `selectedElement` watch at ActionPanel.vue:686-750 auto-starts an action from a board element click (lines 718-749), so the asymmetry the finder describes is real: element clicks can begin an action, choice triggers cannot. The doc comments at useBoardInteraction.ts:170 and ActionPanel.vue:647 explicitly present `triggerChoiceSelect` as the custom-UI choice path, so the silent no-op (no warning when no action is active or when the pick name doesn't match) is a genuine fail-silently pit-of-success issue that violates the project's 'fail fast and loud' principle. I lowered severity from medium to low because the documented primary use (a choice selection mid-action, on the current pick, e.g. suit selection) does work correctly; the silent failure is confined to narrower edge cases — choice-first auto-start and out-of-order pick filling — not the main happy path.

findings.title: actionController.cancel() (the slot-exposed cancel) leaves board-interaction selection/highlight state stale
findings.evidence: `cancel()` clears only controller state — currentAction, args, advanced state — and never touches board interaction (src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:1368-1381); the controller has no reference to it. ActionPanel only fully resets board interaction in its own `cancelAction`/`clearSelection`/`executeAction` (which call `boardInteraction?.clear()`, ActionPanel.vue:1167,443,1255). When a custom UI calls `actionController.cancel()` directly, currentAction goes null: the `watch([currentPick, filteredValidElements])` clears validElements/draggableSelectedElement (ActionPanel.vue:544-548) and `watch(currentAction)` nulls currentAction + choice callback (ActionPanel.vue:664-667), but `selectedElement` and `hoveredChoice` are never cleared, and the availableActions watch's `boardInteraction?.clear()` only runs when the action set actually changes (ActionPanel.vue:497-525), which a manual cancel does not.
findings.whyConcern: After a custom-UI cancel, a board piece can remain visually 'selected'/highlighted via boardInteraction.selectedElement and hoveredChoice, desynced from the now-cleared action.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The slot props hand the custom UI `actionController` and document `cancel()` as the cancel method (useActionController.ts:31 usage notes), but the obvious call leaves stale shared-state highlights — the easy path is subtly wrong, and the correct full reset (`boardInteraction.clear()`) is undocumented for custom UIs.
findings.fixPaths: Have the controller own a reference to (or emit an event consumed to) clear board interaction on cancel, or make ActionPanel's currentAction watch call boardInteraction.clear() (not just setCurrentAction(null)) when the action transitions to null. Files: src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:1368-1381, src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue:649-668.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. useActionController.ts:1368-1381 `cancel()` clears only controller state (currentAction, clearArgs, clearAdvancedState, lastError) and holds no boardInteraction reference. In ActionPanel.vue (actual path /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue): the `watch(currentAction)` at 649-668 only calls setCurrentAction(null)+setChoiceSelectCallback(null); per useBoardInteraction.ts:355-359 setCurrentAction clears currentAction/currentPickIndex/currentPickName only, NOT selectedElement/hoveredChoice. The `watch([currentPick, filteredValidElements])` at 544-548 clears validElements and draggableSelectedElement only. The full reset boardInteraction.clear() (which DOES null selectedElement+hoveredChoice, useBoardInteraction.ts:238-254) runs only inside cancelAction (1167), executeAction finally (1255), startActionConfiguration (1151), and the availableActions watch (519-523) which is gated on shouldClear (action set change) — not triggered by a bare cancel(). selectedElement/hoveredChoice drive real highlight CSS (AutoElement.vue:185,194,1219-1220) and are set during normal flow (AutoElement.vue:552/588/596/803, ActionPanel.vue:1206/1213). So a custom UI that uses boardInteraction for selection/highlight parity (a CLAUDE.md hard rule) and calls the documented actionController.cancel() (useActionController.ts:25-36 wizard API) leaves a stale board highlight. The mechanism and citations are accurate. I downgrade medium→low because the consequence is purely cosmetic, recoverable on next interaction, and requires a specific custom-UI usage combination; it is nonetheless a legitimate pit-of-success trap since the obvious cancel path leaks shared-state highlights and the correct boardInteraction.clear() is undocumented for custom UIs.

findings.title: registerBeforeAutoExecute overwrites instead of registering, silently dropping earlier hooks
findings.evidence: The field comment says hooks 'can be added after creation via registerBeforeAutoExecute' (src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:154), but the function body simply assigns `beforeAutoExecuteHook.value = hook` (useActionController.ts:738-742), and the auto-execute watch invokes only that single ref (useActionController.ts:711-719). The slot exposes one shared actionController to all custom board components.
findings.whyConcern: If more than one component registers a before-auto-execute hook (e.g., separate boards each capturing element positions for FLIP/flying animations), only the last registration runs; the others are silently discarded, so some animations won't capture start positions.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The verb 'register' plus the 'can be added' comment imply additive subscription, leading a developer to assume both hooks fire. The single-slot overwrite is a quiet footgun that only manifests as missing animations.
findings.fixPaths: Either store hooks in an array and await them in sequence, or rename to setBeforeAutoExecute and update the comment to say it replaces. File: src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:154-156,738-742,711-719.
findings.verifierReasoning: All citations confirmed on current source. useActionController.ts:154 comment reads "Registered hooks (can be added after creation via registerBeforeAutoExecute)" — implying additive registration. But the function body at lines 738-742 simply does `beforeAutoExecuteHook.value = hook`, a single-slot overwrite (the ref is declared singular at line 156, not an array). The auto-execute watch at lines 711-719 invokes exactly one ref (`beforeAutoExecuteHook.value`), so only the last registered hook fires. The verb "register" plus the "can be added" comment genuinely imply additive subscription semantics, which the implementation does not provide. This is a real pit-of-success / API-contract mismatch: a second caller silently clobbers the first with no warning, and the symptom (missing FLIP/flying-animation start-position capture) is quiet. The shared actionController is exposed via slot and is used externally (BoardSmithGames/demo-animation/src/ui/components/GameTable.vue:341,350 both call registerBeforeAutoExecute). The multi-component collision scenario is somewhat speculative — current real games register a single hook — so impact is limited, justifying low severity rather than higher. Not an invalid/false-positive: the code does exactly what the finding claims and the naming/comment misleads.

findings.title: AutoUI native drag image is clipped for transformed elements; custom-UI useDragDrop is not — visual parity break
findings.evidence: `useDragDrop.dragProps` detects a CSS transform on the source and builds a transform-stripped clone for a clean drag ghost (src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:186-213). AutoElement's own `handleDragStart` sets the drag data and calls startDrag but never sets a custom drag image or strips transforms (src/ui/components/auto-ui/AutoElement.vue:811-829), so a hovered/translated element drags with a clipped ghost.
findings.whyConcern: The same element dragged from the AutoUI board versus a custom board produces visibly different drag ghosts, contradicting the parity expectation between the two interaction surfaces.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Minor, but a designer comparing AutoUI to a custom board sees inconsistent drag visuals and may waste time chasing a 'bug' that is actually two divergent drag-image code paths.
findings.fixPaths: Extract the transform-aware setDragImage logic from useDragDrop into a shared helper and call it from AutoElement.handleDragStart. Files: src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:186-213, src/ui/components/auto-ui/AutoElement.vue:811-829.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified both citations on current source. useDragDrop.ts:185-213 indeed detects a CSS transform via getComputedStyle, and when transform !== 'none' builds a transform-stripped clone offscreen and passes it to e.dataTransfer.setDragImage(clone, x, y), otherwise uses the element directly — exactly as claimed. AutoElement.vue:811-829 handleDragStart only calls event.dataTransfer.setData(...), sets effectAllowed = 'move', and boardInteraction.startDrag(...); it never inspects the computed transform nor calls setDragImage, so a translated/hovered element on the AutoUI board drags with the browser-default (transform-clipped) ghost while the same element on a custom board using useDragDrop gets the clean clone. So the two interaction surfaces genuinely diverge in drag-ghost appearance. This is a real but purely cosmetic parity gap (no functional/state impact), so low severity is appropriate.

findings.title: GameSession.restore() rebuilds state via action-replay, which the runtime itself documents as unsound — two hosting worlds with opposite correctness guarantees
findings.evidence: GameSession.restore() reconstructs the game with GameRunner.replay() (src/session/game-session.ts:539-546), and StoredGameState (src/session/types.ts:238-266) persists only `actionHistory` — no snapshot, no state, no actionCheckpoints. But GameRunner's own docs (src/runtime/runner.ts:296-303) state replay 'was also unsound: selection-step / pending-completed actions are recorded in NEITHER command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positioned the flow and crashed real games (e.g. MERC's AI dictator Day-1 turn: "Player N is not awaiting action")'. The authoritative, state-direct path GameRunner.fromSnapshot exists and is used by the stateless executor (src/session/stateless-ops.ts:207, 251, 287, 335, 386) and SnapshotSessionHost (persists the full `snapshot` incl. actionCheckpoints, src/session/snapshot-session-host.ts:36), but GameSession never uses it for restore.
findings.whyConcern: A server restart / cold reload of any game that uses multi-step or repeating selections (the entire processSelectionStep path) restores via replay, which omits those mutations and mis-positions the flow — silently producing a board that never existed, or throwing 'not awaiting action'. The exact same library has a sound restore primitive that this path ignores. Additionally, because only actionHistory is persisted, undo/time-travel/rewind are silently dead after any restore (their own error strings admit 'a session cold-restored from action history alone cannot undo across pending mutations', src/session/state-history.ts:295-298).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: GameSession is the prominently exported, documented session API (src/session/index.ts example shows GameSession.create/restore). A developer integrating BoardSmith reaches for the obvious GameSession.restore and gets the worse-guarantee replay path, while the correct snapshot-authoritative restore is only wired into the newer stateless host. The easy path is the wrong path.
findings.fixPaths: Persist a GameStateSnapshot (runner.getSnapshot(), which already carries state+flowState+randomState+actionCheckpoints) in StoredGameState, and make GameSession.restore() call GameRunner.fromSnapshot() instead of GameRunner.replay(). Keep actionHistory only for undo turn-detection. This unifies both hosting worlds on the single authoritative restore primitive.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Every citation checks out against current source.

1. GameSession.restore() (src/session/game-session.ts:533-546) reconstructs the game via GameRunner.replay(...storedState.actionHistory) — confirmed, the unsound replay path, not fromSnapshot.

2. StoredGameState (src/session/types.ts:238-266) persists only actionHistory (line 244) plus lobby/options metadata — no `snapshot`, no `state`, no `actionCheckpoints`, no `flowState`. So a cold restore HAS no choice but to replay. Confirmed.

3. GameRunner's own doc comment (src/runtime/runner.ts:294-305) explicitly states replay "was also unsound: selection-step / pending-completed actions are recorded in NEITHER command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positioned the flow and crashed real games (e.g. MERC's AI dictator Day-1 turn: 'Player N is not awaiting action')." fromSnapshot was created specifically to sidestep this. Confirmed verbatim.

4. fromSnapshot is the state-authoritative primitive (runner.ts:307-349, loads serialized state, restores sequence/RNG/checkpoints, no replay) and is used throughout the stateless executor (src/session/stateless-ops.ts: fromSnapshot at lines 207, 251, 287, 315, 335, 386, 468, 482, 507, 541, 570, 591) and the snapshot host persists the full snapshot incl. actionCheckpoints (src/session/snapshot-session-host.ts:36). Confirmed.

5. state-history.ts:295-298 error string admits "a session cold-restored from action history alone cannot undo across pending mutations." Confirmed.

6. GameSession is a prominently exported, documented public API (src/session/index.ts:110-115 exports it; the file-level example at lines 18-31 leads with GameSession.create). restore() is part of that public surface. The dev-host bridge comment (src/cli/dev-host/bridge.ts:2) describes itself as "the in-process stand-in for ShufflewickPub's GameSession," indicating an external consumer uses GameSession.

The finding is real and well-evidenced: two coexisting, fully-exported session worlds with opposite correctness guarantees, where the obvious/documented one (GameSession.restore) silently takes the replay path the library's own code documents as unsound and proven to crash, while the sound primitive (fromSnapshot) exists but is wired only into the newer snapshot host. That is a genuine pit-of-success violation — the easy path is the wrong path.

Severity downgraded from high to medium: in-repo, GameSession.restore() has NO non-test caller (grep shows only tests in src/session/*.test.ts invoke it), and the codebase is visibly mid-migration toward the snapshot host (stateless-ops + snapshot-session-host now drive dev and the executor). The blast radius is therefore limited to external integrators who choose GameSession over the newer host. Real correctness/trap concern, but not actively breaking an in-repo production path, so medium rather than high.

findings.title: Completed pending/repeating-selection actions are never appended to actionHistory — the recording gap that corrupts replay, undo counts, and AI history
findings.evidence: The only place an action is recorded is GameRunner.performAction → this.actionHistory.push(serializedAction) (src/runtime/runner.ts:199). But actions with repeating/multi-step selections complete through PendingActionManager.#completePendingAction, which calls executor.executePendingAction() + game.continueFlowAfterPendingAction() directly (src/session/pending-action-manager.ts:288-292) — bypassing runner.performAction entirely. executePendingAction (src/engine/action/action.ts:1582-1610) just calls action.execute() and returns; it never serializes or pushes to history. The subsequent `this.#storedState.actionHistory = this.#runner.actionHistory` (pending-action-manager.ts:293) re-points to the same array and adds nothing.
findings.whyConcern: Every multi-step selection action is invisible to actionHistory. Consequences: (1) replay-based restore (see related finding) loses these actions and diverges; (2) a turn whose only action was a multi-step selection yields actionsThisTurn===0 in computeUndoInfo's history-scan fallback, so undo reports 'No actions to undo' even though the player acted; (3) the AI bot reconstructs search state from runner.actionHistory (src/session/stateless-ops.ts:414 createBot(... runner.actionHistory ...)), so it replays from an undercount.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A game designer who builds an action with chooseElements()/repeatUntil (a first-class, encouraged engine feature) silently loses history fidelity for that action — with no error. The framework makes the feature easy to use but quietly drops it from the audit/undo/replay record.
findings.fixPaths: Route pending-action completion through the same recording funnel: have executePendingAction/#completePendingAction serialize the completed action (name, player, collectedArgs) and push it onto runner.actionHistory exactly like performAction does, so history is the single source of truth for what happened. Alternatively, make the snapshot the persisted source of truth (related finding) so the recording gap stops mattering for state, and fix computeUndoInfo to not depend on per-action history entries.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified against current source; all citations are accurate. The only append to actionHistory is runner.ts:199 (`this.actionHistory.push(serializedAction)`) inside performAction — confirmed via grep (other actionHistory references at runner.ts:329/409 are snapshot restore; session lines 293/838/1526 only re-point the array reference). Multi-step/repeating-selection actions complete through PendingActionManager.#completePendingAction (pending-action-manager.ts:288-293), which calls executor.executePendingAction() + game.continueFlowAfterPendingAction() directly, never runner.performAction. executePendingAction (action.ts:1582-1610) just resolves args and calls action.execute(); it never serializes or pushes to history. continueFlowAfterPendingAction (game.ts:1294-1310) only advances the flow engine and has no actionHistory interaction. Line 293 reassigns actionHistory to the same array (no-op append). I confirmed the live session routes single-shot actions through executeAction→performAction (game-session.ts:822, which records at 838) but routes interactive multi-step selections through processSelectionStep→pending manager (game-session.ts:1411), which does NOT record. The downstream consequences are real: AI bot reconstructs from runner.actionHistory (stateless-ops.ts:414) and undo uses computeUndoInfo's fallback history-scan (utils.ts:322) called without moveCount (stateless-ops.ts:345), so a turn whose only action was a multi-step selection undercounts. This is a genuine silent recording gap on a first-class, encouraged engine feature (chooseElements/repeatUntil). I assign high rather than the finder's high-with-full-state-loss framing: the most catastrophic state-loss-on-restore is partially mitigated because the stateful session restores via snapshot/checkpoint at action-count (runner.ts:228 getSnapshot captures trailing pending mutations into actionCheckpoints[len]). But the undo-count bug and AI-history undercount/replay divergence (mcts-bot restoreGame replaying actions via continueFlow misses plain-property mutations) remain real and unmitigated. High is warranted.

findings.title: Undo computes the turn-start index with the heuristic the code itself flags as broken, while the displayed canUndo/turnStartActionIndex use the authoritative moveCount — they disagree
findings.evidence: buildPlayerState feeds the client's canUndo / turnStartActionIndex / actionsThisTurn by calling computeUndoInfo WITH flowState.moveCount (src/session/utils.ts:365-369). But both undo execution paths call computeUndoInfo WITHOUT moveCount: StateHistory.undoToTurnStart (src/session/state-history.ts:270-273) and the stateless undo op (src/session/stateless-ops.ts:345). computeUndoInfo's no-moveCount branch is the backward-scan fallback whose own comment warns: 'This heuristic can fail for games with phases where the same player acts at the end of one phase and start of the next' (src/session/utils.ts:315-318, 296-313).
findings.whyConcern: When moveCount is present (exactly the phase-transition case it was added to fix, e.g. MERC where a player acts at end of Day 1 and start of Day 2), the display says the turn started at index X but undo rewinds to an earlier index Y found by scanning back past the phase boundary. fromCheckpoint(Y) succeeds (a checkpoint exists there), so undo silently restores too far — discarding committed actions from the previous phase/turn that were never meant to be undoable. The user clicks 'undo my turn' and loses a prior turn's work.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The single computeUndoInfo function has two call conventions (with/without moveCount) with different correctness; the two undo entry points both happened to pick the wrong one. Any future undo caller will copy the pattern next to it and inherit the bug. Same source of truth, two answers.
findings.fixPaths: Pass flowState.moveCount into computeUndoInfo at src/session/state-history.ts:270 and src/session/stateless-ops.ts:345 so undo execution uses the identical turn-boundary the UI displayed. Better: make computeUndoInfo require an explicit moveCount/flowState argument (no silent fallback) so a caller cannot accidentally select the broken heuristic.
findings.verifierReasoning: All four file:line citations verified accurate against current source. buildPlayerState (src/session/utils.ts:365-369) passes flowState?.moveCount into computeUndoInfo, and the result drives the client-facing turnStartActionIndex/actionsThisTurn/canUndo (line 372). Both undo-EXECUTION paths omit moveCount: undoToTurnStart (src/session/state-history.ts:270-273) calls computeUndoInfo(actionHistory, flowState.currentPlayer) with no third arg though flowState — which carries moveCount — is in scope (line 264); handleUndo (src/session/stateless-ops.ts:345) calls computeUndoInfo(runner.actionHistory, op.player) with flowState fetched at line 340. In computeUndoInfo, the moveCount branch (utils.ts:296-313) is documented as "the authoritative source" that "correctly handles phase transitions where the same player acts at the end of one phase and start of the next," while the no-moveCount fallback (utils.ts:315-337) carries the comment at 317-318 warning it "can fail for games with phases where the same player acts at the end of one phase and start of the next." So the display and the executed undo use two computations of differing correctness. In the exact phase-transition scenario moveCount was added to fix (e.g. MERC), the backward-scan counts prior-phase same-player actions, yielding a smaller turnStartActionIndex than moveCount; undo then rewinds further than the UI indicated, and GameRunner.fromCheckpoint(turnStartActionIndex) succeeds at that earlier per-action checkpoint, so committed prior-phase actions are silently discarded rather than erroring. moveCount is available in both undo paths but not passed — a genuine, easily-fixed inconsistency. Severity high (not critical): silent data loss on undo, but only manifests in phase-transition games where the same player acts across a boundary, not the common single-phase case. Pit-of-success concern is valid: one function with two call conventions of unequal correctness invites future callers to copy the wrong one.

findings.title: GameSession re-derives errorCode by substring-matching the runner's error string, losing structured codes the runner already knew — NOT_YOUR_TURN is never produced
findings.evidence: GameRunner.performAction returns precise failure reasons as plain strings: 'Player not found' (src/runtime/runner.ts:131-133), 'Game is not awaiting input' (runner.ts:142-145), `Not ${name}'s turn` (runner.ts:148-152) — but no errorCode. GameSession.performAction then guesses the code by substring: .includes('not available') → ACTION_NOT_AVAILABLE, .includes('not found') → ACTION_NOT_FOUND, .includes('Invalid selection') → INVALID_PICK (src/session/game-session.ts:824-834). 'Not Alice's turn' matches none → errorCode stays undefined; 'Player not found' matches 'not found' → mislabeled ACTION_NOT_FOUND.
findings.whyConcern: The runner distinguishes these cases internally but discards the distinction, and GameSession can't reliably reconstruct it from prose. A turn violation therefore returns no NOT_YOUR_TURN code, and a player-not-found returns ACTION_NOT_FOUND. The types.ts doc example (src/session/types.ts:27-37) literally tells clients to `switch (result.errorCode) { case ErrorCode.NOT_YOUR_TURN: ... }` — that case is dead code for real turn errors.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The framework documents errorCode-based handling as the recommended pattern, but the codes are produced by fragile string-sniffing that silently fails. Clients following the documented pattern fall through to default and show a generic error; designers won't notice because the string message is still present.
findings.fixPaths: Have GameRunner.performAction return a structured errorCode alongside the message (it already knows the exact branch), and have GameSession pass it through instead of re-inferring. Make ErrorCode the single source of truth from the point the error originates.
findings.verifierReasoning: All citations verified on current source. runner.ts:130-153 returns the three failure reasons as plain strings ('Player not found', 'Game is not awaiting input', `Not ${name}'s turn`) with no errorCode. game-session.ts:824-834 re-derives errorCode by substring matching only 'not available', 'not found', and 'Invalid selection'. The turn-violation string "Not X's turn" matches none of these, so errorCode stays undefined — ErrorCode.NOT_YOUR_TURN (defined at types.ts:42) is never produced. types.ts:26-32 documents `switch (result.errorCode) { case ErrorCode.NOT_YOUR_TURN: ... }` as the recommended client pattern, making that case dead for real turn errors. The runner's 'Player not found' also includes 'not found' and would be mislabeled ACTION_NOT_FOUND (though the explicit INVALID_PLAYER guard at 818-820 covers the in-range player check, the structured-code-loss problem and the NOT_YOUR_TURN omission are both real and reachable). Genuine pit-of-success defect: documented errorCode handling silently fails due to fragile string-sniffing. Medium severity — non-crashing but the recommended pattern dead-ends to default.

findings.title: Client SDK mints player-identity capability tokens with Math.random()
findings.evidence: src/client/client.ts:572-577 — generatePlayerId() builds the default playerId as `${Date.now().toString(36)}-${Math.random().toString(36).substring(2,10)}` and the constructor uses it as the client's identity (client.ts:37). src/types/protocol.ts:44-51 documents that playerId is a per-seat *capability*: "it is used as the identity proof on WebSocket connect and for host-only authorization checks" and is masked from other players for security.
findings.whyConcern: The playerId is the only auth token: connecting, claiming/leaving seats, kicking players, and changing game options (client.ts:461,508) all authenticate solely by this string. It is generated from a fully predictable timestamp prefix plus ~8 base36 chars of non-cryptographic Math.random(). Math.random() is not a CSPRNG and is seedable/predictable, so another player's identity proof is guessable/brute-forceable — enabling seat hijacking, impersonation, and host-only actions (kick, change options).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The easy path (instantiate MeepleClient and use the auto-generated id) gives every game a weak, guessable identity token. A developer has to *deliberately* call setPlayerId() with their own strong id to be safe — the secure path is the hard path, exactly inverted.
findings.fixPaths: src/client/client.ts:572-577 — generate the id with crypto.randomUUID() (available in modern browsers and Node 16+), falling back to crypto.getRandomValues() rather than Math.random(); drop the predictable Date.now() prefix or keep it only as a non-security display hint.
findings.verifierReasoning: Citations confirmed on current source. src/client/client.ts:572-577 generatePlayerId() = `${Date.now().toString(36)}-${Math.random().toString(36).substring(2,10)}`; constructor at client.ts:37 assigns it as identity. src/types/protocol.ts:44-52 explicitly documents playerId as a per-seat security capability used as identity proof and for host-only authorization, masked from other players. The capability is genuinely enforced as the sole auth token: src/session/lobby-manager.ts uses #isHost(playerId) to gate add/remove/toggle/kick slots (lines 437,490,565,769), compares slot.playerId===playerId for claim/leave (line 203), and slot.playerId===creatorId for host detection (line 133). setPlayerId() at client.ts:542 is the opt-in strong-id path, confirming the pit-of-success inversion: the easy default produces a non-CSPRNG token, security requires deliberate effort. Real defect — Math.random() is not a CSPRNG and crypto.randomUUID() is the trivial correct default for an identity capability. Two caveats lowering severity from the claimed high to medium: (1) the finder's references to a network server/host-only checks pre-date the F8/F9 deletion of src/server and src/worker — those dirs no longer exist; enforcement now lives in-process in session. (2) Practical exploitability is weaker than claimed: ~41 bits of truncated Math.random entropy, ids are masked from other players, and cross-client PRNG state isn't shared, so remote brute-force seat hijacking is non-trivial. Still a legitimate insecure-default capability-token defect with a trivial fix.

findings.title: assertFlowState treats expected `actions` as a subset, not the exact set
findings.evidence: src/testing/assertions.ts:96-101 — for expected.actions it only computes `missingActions = expected.actions.filter(a => !actual.actions?.includes(a))` and fails únicamente if something expected is missing. Extra available actions never fail the assertion. The doc example (assertions.ts:58-62) lists `actions: ['move','attack']` implying the complete available set.
findings.whyConcern: A test asserting `actions: ['move']` passes even when the engine actually offers `['move','attack','win_instantly']`. Tests meant to lock down which actions are legal in a given flow state silently accept extra (possibly illegal) actions — the exact regression these assertions exist to catch slips through green.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The natural reading of `assertFlowState(g,{actions:[...]})` is "these are the available actions." A game author writes the list expecting exactness and gets a false sense of coverage; the wrong assertion is the easy one to write.
findings.fixPaths: src/testing/assertions.ts:96-101 — also report unexpected extras (set-equality), or add an explicit `exactActions` vs `includesActions` distinction so exact matching is the default/obvious choice.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Citation is accurate. At src/testing/assertions.ts:96-101 the `actions` check only computes `missingActions = expected.actions.filter(a => !actual.actions?.includes(a))` and pushes an error solely when something expected is absent. There is no reverse check for extra/unexpected actions, so `assertFlowState(g, {actions:['move']})` passes even if the engine offers `['move','attack',...]`. This is genuine subset (not exact-set) matching. The JSDoc example at lines 57-62 lists `actions: ['move','attack']`, which a reader naturally interprets as the complete available set, reinforcing the pit-of-success concern that an author expects exactness and gets silent under-coverage. The finding's claims are all confirmed against current source.

I downgrade severity from medium to low: (1) this is test-utility code, not runtime game logic — the failure mode is weaker test confidence, not incorrect game behavior; (2) the interface doc comment at line 19 literally reads "Actions that should be available," which is arguably legitimate subset semantics, so the behavior is partially defensible rather than a clear bug; (3) dedicated exact-direction helpers already exist — `assertActionNotAvailable` (lines 308-326) lets authors lock down that a specific action is illegal. The real-but-bounded risk is the mismatch between the subset implementation and the exact-set impression created by the example, which is a valid pit-of-success ergonomics gap worth fixing (e.g., adding an `extraActions` check or an `exact` option), but its blast radius is limited to test strictness.

findings.title: createSeededRandom() silently falls back to Math.random() when no seed is given
findings.evidence: src/utils/random.ts:189-191 — `export function createSeededRandom(seed?: string) { const instance = new SeededRandom(seed ?? Math.random().toString(36).substring(2)); ... }`. The seed parameter is optional and the fallback seed is non-deterministic.
findings.whyConcern: The entire purpose of this module (random.ts:1-19) is deterministic, reproducible randomness for replay/testing/AI cloning. Making the seed optional with a Math.random() fallback means the no-argument call produces a generator that is NOT reproducible — a different sequence every run — while still being named/typed as a SeededRandom. This also directly violates the repo rule "Never use dummy data, fallbacks, or other hacks" (CLAUDE.md).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: `createSeededRandom()` is the shortest call and looks correct, but yields non-reproducible output; a developer relying on it for deterministic behavior (e.g. AI weight init, simulation) gets silently broken reproducibility. The right path (always pass a seed) should be the only path.
findings.fixPaths: src/utils/random.ts:189 — make seed required (remove the `?` and the Math.random() fallback). Callers that want a fresh seed must pass one explicitly. ai-trainer already always passes a seed, so the breakage surface is small.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Citation verified exact. src/utils/random.ts:189-191 is `export function createSeededRandom(seed?: string): () => number { const instance = new SeededRandom(seed ?? Math.random().toString(36).substring(2)); return () => instance.next(); }` — the seed param is genuinely optional and the fallback is non-deterministic Math.random(). The module's stated purpose (random.ts:4-7, 24-25) is deterministic, reproducible randomness, and the SeededRandom constructor itself (line 59) correctly makes seed mandatory. So the convenience factory contradicts both the module intent and the CLAUDE.md "no fallbacks" rule by making `createSeededRandom()` compile/run while silently yielding a non-reproducible generator typed as seeded. This is a legitimate pit-of-success design flaw: the shortest call looks correct but breaks reproducibility.

However, I downgrade severity from medium to low because the problem is purely latent: every real caller passes a seed (weight-evolver.ts:179,202 and parallel-trainer.ts:451,474 all use `${this.config.seed ?? 'evolution'}-...`, always a defined string; the evolution.test.ts calls all pass explicit seeds). The Math.random() fallback branch is never reached in current code, so there is no active reproducibility bug — only a footgun for future callers. Real but low-impact API hardening issue (drop the optional param / fallback so the wrong path doesn't typecheck).

findings.title: simulateRandomGames cannot generate action arguments and silently swallows failed actions
findings.evidence: src/testing/random-simulation.ts:204 — getAvailableActions returns every action with `args: {}` (comment: "real impl would generate valid args"). simulateSingleGame at random-simulation.ts:147-152 increments only on success and does nothing on failure (comment: "This is simplified"). The doc example at random-simulation.ts:229 asserts `expect(results.completed).toBe(100)`.
findings.whyConcern: Any game whose actions take arguments (i.e. nearly all real games) will have every doAction() fail because args are always empty. Because the loop keeps re-selecting the same failing no-arg actions while isAwaitingInput stays true, the game neither progresses nor errors — it spins until the timeout and reports completed:0 / timedOut. The harness thus produces a confident-looking pass/fail signal that is meaningless for arg-based games.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer follows the documented pattern, sees 0 completions or all-timeouts, and concludes their game is broken (or, for the rare all-no-arg game, gets false confidence that random play is exercised when arg paths are never touched). Either way the testing utility lures the user to a wrong conclusion.
findings.fixPaths: src/testing/random-simulation.ts:191-209 — either drive argument generation through the engine's action-introspection/pick metadata (ActionMetadata/PickMetadata in types/protocol.ts) so valid args are produced, or fail loudly when an action requires args instead of silently retrying. At minimum, track consecutive failures and surface them rather than masking via timeout.
findings.verifierReasoning: All citations are accurate on current source. random-simulation.ts:204 returns every available action with `args: {}` (comment: "real impl would generate valid args"). random-simulation.ts:147-152 increments actionCount only on success and does nothing/tracks nothing on failure (comment "This is simplified"). random-simulation.ts:229 documents `expect(results.completed).toBe(100)` for GoFishGame. The finder's failure mechanism is provably real: because actionCount only increments on success (line 147-148), the maxActions guard (line 115) can never fire for arg-requiring games; isAwaitingInput stays true so the line-121 stuck-break never fires; the loop's only exit is the timeout at line 109. So an arg-based game spins ~5s and reports completed:0 / timedOut. doAction (test-game.ts:184) returns `{ success }`, and its own doc examples (test-game.ts:46, 250) plus the GoFishGame example show actions requiring args (target, rank) — which the simulator always supplies as `{}`, guaranteeing failure. The utility is exported public API (src/testing/index.ts:53) with a documented, runnable example that is wrong for essentially every real game, luring designers into concluding their game is broken (or false confidence for the rare no-arg game). I set severity medium rather than the finder's medium-but-arguable framing because it is a test-only helper, not production game logic, but the misleading documented example and pit-of-success harm are genuine.

findings.title: Random-simulation reports a 'seed' that cannot reproduce the failure it found
findings.evidence: src/testing/random-simulation.ts:254 — `const seed = `sim-${playerCount}-${i}-${Date.now()}``. The seed embeds Date.now(), and SimulateRandomGamesOptions (random-simulation.ts:17-28) exposes no way to feed a seed back in. The per-game seed is surfaced on SingleGameResult.seed (random-simulation.ts:51).
findings.whyConcern: The point of seeding a fuzz/simulation harness is that when a crash is found you can replay that exact game by its seed. Because the seed includes wall-clock time and the public API accepts no seed input, re-running simulateRandomGames with identical parameters produces entirely different games, and the reported seed string has no public entry point to replay it. Found bugs are effectively non-reproducible.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The result object advertising a `seed` field strongly implies reproducibility; a developer records the seed from a crash, finds no way to re-run it, and cannot debug the failure — the obvious path leads to a dead end.
findings.fixPaths: src/testing/random-simulation.ts:17-28 add a `seed`/`baseSeed` option, and at line 254 derive per-game seeds deterministically from it (e.g. `${baseSeed}-${playerCount}-${i}`) with no Date.now(); expose a single-seed replay entry point.
findings.verifierReasoning:
All cited file:line claims are accurate on current source at /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/testing/random-simulation.ts:

- Line 254: `const seed = `sim-${playerCount}-${i}-${Date.now()}`` — the per-game seed string does embed wall-clock time (Date.now()), confirmed verbatim.
- Lines 17-28: `SimulateRandomGamesOptions` exposes only count, playerCounts, timeout, maxActions, onGameComplete — there is NO field to feed a seed back in. Confirmed.
- Line 51: `SingleGameResult.seed: string` is surfaced on every result, confirmed.

The problem is real. `simulateRandomGames` IS public API (exported from src/testing/index.ts:53). The internal `simulateSingleGame` that consumes a seed is marked @internal and is NOT exported (the only exported `simulateSingleGame` is an unrelated function in src/ai-trainer). So a developer who hits a crash, records `result.seed`, has no public entry point to replay that exact game. Even worse, because the seed embeds Date.now(), re-running with identical SimulateRandomGamesOptions parameters generates entirely different seed strings and different games — so accidental "just run it again" reproduction also fails. While the SeededRandom inside simulateSingleGame is deterministic for a given seed string (so reproduction is theoretically possible if the harness accepted the seed), the public surface offers no path to do so. This is a genuine pit-of-success trap: the `seed` field strongly advertises reproducibility, yet the obvious developer path (record seed -> replay) is a dead end.

Severity medium rather than the finder's medium-vs-higher: this is a test/fuzz utility, not production runtime, so blast radius is limited to developer debugging workflow. But for a fuzz harness, reproducibility of found crashes is the core value proposition, so the defect meaningfully undermines the tool's purpose. Medium is appropriate.

findings.title: no-network eslint rule misses all module-import network paths (node:http/https/net/dgram)
findings.evidence: src/eslint-plugin/rules/no-network.ts:24-56 only flags a bare `fetch()` Identifier call and `new XMLHttpRequest()/new WebSocket()`. It has no ImportDeclaration/require/ImportExpression handling. The companion no-filesystem rule does check imports (no-filesystem.ts:40-69) but its forbidden list (no-filesystem.ts:25: `['fs','fs/promises','path','child_process','os']`) excludes every networking module.
findings.whyConcern: A game can perform real network I/O via `import https from 'node:https'` / `node:http` / `node:net` / `node:dgram` / `node:http2`, or `window.fetch(...)` (member expression), and neither rule fires — yet sandbox-scan.ts and validateSecurity advertise these rules as the single source of truth that 'a fetch() in a shared module is caught.' The enforcement the docs claim does not actually exist for these vectors, so non-deterministic/network code passes `boardsmith validate` and `boardsmith lint`.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The lint/validate green check tells a game author their game is sandbox-clean when it can still hit the network through node builtins, breaking replay/multiplayer determinism and defeating the security guarantee the rule promises.
findings.fixPaths: src/eslint-plugin/rules/no-network.ts — add ImportDeclaration/ImportExpression/require handling mirroring no-filesystem.ts, blocking `http`,`https`,`net`,`dgram`,`http2`,`tls`,`dns` (with and without `node:` prefix); also flag member-expression `fetch` (e.g. window.fetch/globalThis.fetch) like no-timers.ts already does for timers.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed on current source. no-network.ts:27-55 only flags a bare `fetch` Identifier CallExpression and `new XMLHttpRequest()/new WebSocket()`; it has no ImportDeclaration/require/ImportExpression handling, and the CallExpression check requires callee.type==='Identifier', so `import https from 'node:https'`/`node:http`/`node:net`/`node:dgram`/`node:http2`, `require('node:net')`, dynamic `import('node:net')`, AND member-expression calls like `window.fetch(...)`/`globalThis.fetch(...)` all pass. no-filesystem.ts does check imports (lines 40-69) but its forbiddenModules list (line 25: ['fs','fs/promises','path','child_process','os']) contains no networking modules, so it doesn't cover the gap. sandbox-scan.ts:1-11 explicitly documents itself as 'the single source of truth' and claims 'a fetch() in a shared module is caught,' and SANDBOX_RULES (lines 31-37) wires only these rules; validate/lint both delegate here and there is no other network-enforcement layer (in-process runtime sandbox was previously removed per F13). So real network/non-deterministic code passes the green check, breaking the advertised guarantee. The finder's citations are accurate. I rate medium: genuine gap in a tool documented as the sole guardrail, with a trivial window.fetch member-expression bypass, though exploiting node networking builtins requires deliberate imports rather than accidental misuse.

findings.title: WebSocketMessage is a stringly-typed bag where every payload field is optional
findings.evidence: src/types/protocol.ts:227-265 — a single interface with a 17-value `type` union and every other field optional (`action?`, `seat?`, `name?`, `ready?`, `isAI?`, `gameOptions?`, ...). Nothing ties `type:'action'` to a required `action`, or `type:'claimSeat'` to a required `seat`/`name`.
findings.whyConcern: Invalid messages are fully representable and type-check clean: `{type:'action'}` (no action), `{type:'kickPlayer'}` (no seat), `{type:'claimSeat'}` (no name/seat). The types provide zero help that each message kind has required fields, pushing all validation to runtime on the server.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A client author writing message-sending code gets autocomplete that makes every field look optional for every type, so it's easy to ship a structurally-invalid message that compiles fine and only fails at runtime. A discriminated union would make invalid messages unrepresentable.
findings.fixPaths: src/types/protocol.ts:227-265 — replace the single interface with a discriminated union: one interface per `type` literal carrying exactly its required fields (e.g. `{type:'action';action:string;args:Record<string,unknown>;requestId?:string}` | `{type:'claimSeat';seat:number;name:string}` | ...).
findings.verifierReasoning: Read src/types/protocol.ts:227-265 directly. The cited code is exactly as described: a single `WebSocketMessage` interface whose `type` field is a string union (16 members — action, ping, getState, getLobby, claimSeat, joinLobby, updateName, setReady, addSlot, removeSlot, setSlotAI, leaveSeat, kickPlayer, updatePlayerOptions, updateSlotPlayerOptions, updateGameOptions; the finder said 17, an immaterial miscount), and every payload field (action?, args?, requestId?, seat?, name?, ready?, isAI?, aiLevel?, playerOptions?, gameOptions?) is optional. Nothing in the type ties `type:'action'` to a required `action`, or `type:'claimSeat'` to a required `seat`/`name`. So invalid messages like `{type:'action'}` or `{type:'claimSeat'}` are fully representable and type-check clean, pushing all per-type field validation to runtime. This is a genuine pit-of-success type-design issue — a discriminated union keyed on `type` would make invalid combinations unrepresentable and give correct autocomplete per message kind. The finding is real and accurately cited. I concur with low severity: it is a type-ergonomics/correctness-by-construction concern, not a runtime bug (the server presumably validates), so impact is limited to authoring safety rather than shipped behavior.
